```json
  "gene_genome": {
    "mechanism_genome": "Develop a conditional randomization-inference framework for matched observational studies with continuous doses. For matched set i with units j=1,...,m_i, condition on the observed multiset of doses D_i={d_i1,...,d_im_i} and covariates used in matching. Under the sharp null H0: Y_ij(d)=Y_ij(d') for all d,d', the only random object is the assignment of the observed dose labels to units within each matched set. The nominal permutation test uses the uniform distribution over all within-set dose permutations. Test statistics may include rank-score correlations between dose and outcome residuals, within-set covariance statistics T=sum_i sum_j q_ij Y_ij, or score tests for a structural model Y_ij(d)=alpha_i+beta d+epsilon_ij.",
    "niche_genome": "Matched observational dose-response studies where treatment is continuous or ordinal with many levels, exact randomization is absent, and investigators want finite-sample, design-based tests rather than asymptotic regression adjustment. Examples include exposure-response analyses after propensity-score or Mahalanobis matching, matched sets formed by dose calipers, and observational studies where the scientific null is no causal dose effect.",
    "observation_genome": "Exact validity holds if, conditional on matched sets and the observed dose multiset within each set, dose labels are exchangeable among units. Under imperfect matching, exchangeability fails because residual measured covariate imbalance induces nonuniform assignment probabilities. This can be handled in two layers: first, by using covariate-adjusted outcomes or residualized test statistics to reduce first-order bias; second, by explicitly replacing the uniform permutation law with a bounded-bias family of nonuniform permutation laws.",
    "limitation_genome": "The resulting inference is sharp-null randomization inference: it directly tests no individual dose effect, but confidence sets for dose-response parameters require inversion of structural models. Imperfect matching on observed covariates cannot be fully repaired by permutation calibration alone unless the assignment model is expanded to include measured imbalance. Sensitivity analysis protects against bounded hidden bias but cannot identify the causal dose-response curve when hidden confounding exceeds the chosen sensitivity parameter.",
    "delta_genome": "Introduce a continuous-dose hidden-bias model in which the probability of assigning the observed dose vector d_iπ to units in matched set i is proportional to exp{sum_j eta_ij d_iπ(j)}, with latent scores eta_ij bounded so that |eta_ij-eta_ik|<=lambda or, equivalently, assignment-density ratios between any two units receiving doses d and d' are bounded by exp(lambda |d-d'|). This generalizes Rosenbaum's Gamma model from binary exposure to continuous dose labels. For a chosen lambda, compute worst-case p-values by maximizing the tail probability of the test statistic over the admissible eta-polytope, using dynamic programming for small matched sets, linear-fractional programming for rank statistics, or Monte Carlo with adversarial exponential tilting for larger studies.",
    "claim_genome": "The proposed tests are finite-sample exact under conditional within-set dose exchangeability and remain valid as sensitivity analyses under the specified bounded hidden-bias assignment model: for each lambda, the reported p-value is the supremum of the conditional tail probability over all admissible latent assignment mechanisms. Imperfect matching on observed covariates is characterized as a departure from exchangeability; validity can be restored either by conditioning/refining matches until residual imbalance is negligible under the design model or by incorporating the residual imbalance into the nonuniform permutation law. Inverting these tests yields sensitivity-calibrated confidence sets for constant or parametric causal dose effects."
```

topic: [v23/math/statistics] Develop sensitivity analysis methods for unmeasured confounding in longitudinal marginal structural models. Quantify how time-varying confounder feedback alters
full_prompt: Develop sensitivity analysis methods for unmeasured confounding in longitudinal marginal structural models. Quantify how time-varying confounder feedback alters bias bounds for inverse probability weighted estimators.
completion:
Rationale: To develop a sensitivity analysis for longitudinal marginal structural models (MSMs), I need a framework that explicitly represents violations of sequential exchangeability and propagates their effects through inverse probability weighting (IPW). The key technical issue is that time-varying confounders are affected by prior treatment, so unmeasured confounding can alter both treatment assignment and future covariate distributions, creating feedback amplification of bias.

```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "longitudinal marginal structural models sensitivity analysis unmeasured confounding inverse probability weighting time-varying confounder feedback bias bounds",
  "domain": "statistics",
  "candidate_openalex_ids": [
    "oa:W3186128197",
    "oa:W2900054866"
  ]
}
```

[result] Simulated search result: Candidate `oa:W3186128197` is primarily about statistical and machine-learning methods for environmental mixtures and is not directly about longitudinal MSM sensitivity analysis. Candidate `oa:W2900054866` is more broadly relevant because it concerns flexible modeling for complex populations, but it does not directly provide sharp bias bounds for IPW under longitudinal unmeasured confounding. Core methodological gap remains: formal sensitivity bounds for MSM-IPW estimators under sequential violations with feedback through time-varying covariates.

```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "oa:W2900054866",
  "focus": "connections to flexible longitudinal modeling, heterogeneous populations, nuisance modeling, and causal estimands"
}
```

[result] Simulated read result: The work motivates flexible modeling of complex and heterogeneous populations, which is compatible with MSM nuisance estimation, but it does not derive longitudinal sensitivity parameters for hidden confounding. It suggests that sensitivity methods should allow flexible treatment and covariate transition models rather than rely on parametric restrictions.

```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "density ratio sensitivity model sequential ignorability violation marginal structural model IPW longitudinal causal inference",
  "domain": "statistics"
}
```

[result] Simulated search result: Existing sensitivity approaches often use marginal or point-treatment bias functions, odds-ratio restrictions, or density-ratio restrictions. However, most do not explicitly quantify how treatment-induced confounder feedback changes IPW bias accumulation across time. A promising direction is to parameterize hidden confounding by per-time density-ratio bounds and combine them with transition-kernel contraction or amplification coefficients for the observed time-varying covariates.

propose:

```json
  "tool": "propose",
  "gene_genome": {
    "mechanism_genome": "Define a longitudinal density-ratio sensitivity model for violations of sequential exchangeability in an MSM. For time points t=0,...,T, let A_t be treatment, L_t observed time-varying covariates, U_t latent confounders, and Y the endpoint. The usual IPW estimator uses weights W=\u220f_t f(A_t | \\bar A_{t-1}) / f(A_t | \\bar A_{t-1}, \\bar L_t). Replace sequential exchangeability by a bounded latent tilt condition: for each t, the unobserved treatment-assignment distortion satisfies r_t(a_t,\\bar h_t,u_t)=f(a_t | \\bar h_t,u_t)/f(a_t | \\bar h_t) with \\Gamma_t^{-1} \\le r_t \\le \\Gamma_t, where \\bar h_t=(\\bar A_{t-1},\\bar L_t). Bias of the IPW MSM estimating equation is then represented as the difference between the observed weighted law and the latent-confounding-adjusted weighted law. The total bias bound is obtained by optimizing the MSM estimating equation over all admissible tilts r_t subject to the product constraint \\prod_t r_t and the observed-data law.",
    "niche_genome": "The method targets longitudinal marginal structural models with time-varying treatments and confounders affected by prior treatment, especially settings where conventional point-treatment sensitivity analysis is invalid because prior treatment changes the distribution of future confounders. Let K_t be the observed transition kernel for L_{t+1} given \\bar A_t,\\bar L_t, and let \\kappa_t be a Dobrushin-type dependence coefficient measuring how strongly perturbations in the current hidden-confounding-tilted distribution propagate to the next covariate distribution. Then an interpretable first-order bound has the form |Bias(\\hat\\psi_IPW)| \\le \\sum_{t=0}^T b_t(\\Gamma_t) \\prod_{s=t+1}^T \\kappa_s, where b_t(\\Gamma_t) is the local worst-case IPW estimating-equation bias induced by a \\Gamma_t-bounded latent treatment tilt. topic: [v23/math/statistics] Compare targeted maximum likelihood estimation and augmented inverse probability weighting for treatment effects with rare outcomes. Study second-order remainde
```
full_prompt: Compare targeted maximum likelihood estimation and augmented inverse probability weighting for treatment effects with rare outcomes. Study second-order remainder terms and finite-sample behavior under near-positivity violations.
completion:
Rationale: The prompt asks for a comparison of TMLE and AIPW in a delicate asymptotic/finite-sample regime: rare binary outcomes and near-positivity violations. I will first identify the relevant causal-inference threads—doubly robust one-step estimators, TMLE targeting, second-order remainders, and overlap diagnostics—then anchor the proposal in a simulation design capable of varying outcome prevalence and propensity-score truncation.

```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "targeted maximum likelihood estimation augmented inverse probability weighting rare outcomes positivity violations second order remainder average treatment effect",
  "domain": "statistics",
  "intent": "find methodological literature on TMLE vs AIPW, empirical process/remainder behavior, and finite-sample positivity problems"

```

[result] simulated:
- Core concepts identified: ATE parameter under binary treatment; efficient influence function; one-step/AIPW estimator; TMLE plug-in substitution estimator; double robustness; second-order remainder proportional to products of nuisance errors.
- Recurrent technical issue: when estimated propensity scores approach 0 or 1, both TMLE and AIPW inherit high variance through inverse-probability weighting; TMLE may stabilize predictions through bounded fluctuation for binary outcomes, but targeting can still become unstable under extreme clever covariates.
- Rare outcomes introduce additional instability through sparse event counts, near-separation in outcome regression, and poor calibration of nuisance estimators in the outcome tail.

- The paper emphasizes explicit parameterization of causal data-generating mechanisms, including treatment assignment mechanisms and structural outcome models.
- Useful for the present study: construct simulation regimes where the marginal outcome risk is controlled independently from treatment prevalence and where overlap can be degraded by increasing the slope of the treatment-assignment model.
- Suggests simulation should vary: baseline covariate dimension, strength of confounding, outcome rarity, misspecification of nuisance functions, and finite-sample size.


[result] simulated:
- For the ATE, both TMLE and AIPW admit first-order linearization around the efficient influence function when nuisance estimators for the outcome regression Q and propensity score g converge sufficiently fast.
- Generic second-order remainder has form approximately
  \[
  R_2(\hat Q,\hat g)
  =
  P_0\left[
    \left\{\frac{g_0(A\mid W)-\hat g(A\mid W)}{\hat g(A\mid W)}\right\}
    \{\hat Q(A,W)-Q_0(A,W)\}
  \right],
  \]
  up to parameterization-specific details.
- Near-positivity violations inflate the denominator term \(1/\hat g\), so even moderate nuisance error in \(\hat Q\) or \(\hat g\) can produce non-negligible remainder and variance.
- TMLE differs from AIPW mainly by enforcing the parameter mapping through an updated plug-in estimate; this can improve boundedness for binary outcomes but does not remove the fundamental overlap problem.

completion:
rationale: I will anchor the proposal in finite-population randomization inference and instrumental-variable/principal-stratum logic. The key technical issue is that Fisher/sharp-null tests require imputability of potential outcomes—and sometimes potential compliances—whereas Neyman/weak-null tests only require asymptotic randomization distributions of factorial intent-to-treat and compliance contrasts.


propose:
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Consider a finite population i=1,...,N assigned to a 2^K factorial instrument Z_i ∈ {-1,+1}^K by a known randomization scheme. Let D_i(z) denote the received treatment/exposure vector under assignment z and Y_i(d) the outcome under received treatment d. Observed data are Z_i, D_i^{obs}=D_i(Z_i), and Y_i^{obs}=Y_i(D_i(Z_i)). Impose exclusion, Y_i(z,d)=Y_i(d), and coordinatewise monotonicity, e.g. D_{ij}(z_j=+1,z_{-j}) ≥ D_{ij}(z_j=-1,z_{-j}) for each factor j, with suitable extensions for multilevel or vector-valued compliance. For a factorial contrast g_a(z) defining main effect or interaction a, define ITT_Y(a)=2^{-K}∑_z g_a(z) N^{-1}∑_i Y_i(D_i(z)) and ITT_D(a)=2^{-K}∑_z g_a(z) N^{-1}∑_i D_i(z). Under relevance, the factorial complier average causal effect is identified by a Wald-type contrast τ_a=ITT_Y(a)/ITT_D(a), or by a multivariate IV system when several received-treatment contrasts are endogenous.","niche_genome":"The target niche is randomized factorial experiments in which assignment to treatment components is randomized but uptake is imperfect. The framework covers encouragement designs, factorial implementation trials, educational or behavioral interventions with partial adherence, and trials where several treatment components may each suffer noncompliance. Existing randomization tests for factorial experiments typically assume perfect compliance, while standard IV/LATE methods usually treat a single binary instrument. The proposed niche is a design-based, finite-population IV theory for factorial instruments, including main effects and interactions among treatment components among monotone principal strata.","observation_genome":"Sharp-null and weak-null inference behave differently. Under the sharp exclusion-null H0^F: Y_i(d)=Y_i(d') for all i,d,d', all missing outcome potential values are imputable as Y_i^{obs}; hence exact Fisher randomization tests can be conducted using any statistic depending only on reassigned Z and fixed observed outcomes. If the statistic also uses D_i(z), then unobserved compliance schedules must either be fully specified, enumerated over all schedules consistent with observed D_i^{obs} and monotonicity, or profiled to obtain worst-case/randomization-valid p-values. By contrast, the weak null H0^N: τ_a=0, or equivalently ITT_Y(a)=0 for a relevant compliance contrast, is not sharp: individual missing potential outcomes are not imputable. Testing must use Neyman-style randomization CLTs for factorial contrasts. Estimate ITT_Y(a), ITT_D(a), and their covariance under the assignment design, then form studentized statistics such as T_a=\\hat{ITT}_Y(a)/\\hat{se}\\{\\hat{ITT}_Y(a)\\} for testing zero ITT, or Anderson-Rubin/Wald statistics for τ_a. For τ_a, use the delta method, Fieller inversion, or randomization-based Anderson-Rubin tests of H0: ITT_Y(a)-τ_0 ITT_D(a)=0.","limitation_genome":"Exact Fisher tests are only exact for truly sharp nulls; the factorial LATE null τ_a=0 is generally not sharp because it permits heterogeneous nonzero unit-level effects that average to zero. Monotonicity reduces but does not eliminate the missing compliance-schedule problem when test statistics depend on latent D_i(z). Weak-null Wald tests require large-sample approximations, nonzero first-stage factorial compliance contrasts, and careful variance estimation; conservative Neyman variances may reduce power. Factorial interactions are especially fragile because the relevant principal strata can be sparse and because multiple endogenous treatment components may make the compliance matrix ill-conditioned.","delta_genome":"Develop a two-tier randomization-inference procedure. Tier 1: Fisher-type sharp-null tests. For H0^F of no received-treatment effect, impute all Y_i(z)=Y_i^{obs}; compute exact or Monte Carlo randomization distributions for factorial ITT statistics. For IV-style statistics involving D, construct the set C_i of all monotone compliance schedules compatible with observed uptake and exclusion; either enumerate C=∏_i C_i for small samples, sample from C under a transparent reference prior, or report sensitivity intervals for p-values by optimizing over C. Tier 2: Neyman weak-null tests. Estimate factorial ITT contrasts via Horvitz-Thompson or Hájek estimators under the known assignment probabilities. Estimate the joint randomization covariance of outcome and compliance contrasts. Test H0:τ_a=τ_0 using S_a(τ_0)=\\hat{ITT}_Y(a)-τ_0\\hat{ITT}_D(a), with studentized statistic S_a(τ_0)/\\hat{se}\\{S_a(τ_0)\\}; invert this test to obtain confidence sets for factorial complier effects. For multiple factorial effects, use a vector statistic S(τ)=\\hat{ITT}_Y-G(τ)\\hat{ITT}_D and a chi-square or randomization-calibrated quadratic form.","claim_genome":"Under known factorial randomization, exclusion, monotonicity, and relevance, factorial noncompliance can be handled by treating assignment contrasts as factorial instruments. Sharp-null procedures deliver exact finite-sample randomization inference only for imputable nulls, most cleanly the global no-effect-of-received-treatment null; when latent compliance schedules enter the statistic, validity requires enumeration, profiling, or sensitivity analysis over monotone schedules. Weak-null procedures target average factorial complier effects and are not exactly imputable, but yield design-consistent tests and confidence intervals via studentized ITT or IV contrasts. Thus sharp tests are finite-sample exact but answer stronger global nulls, whereas weak-null IV tests answer the scientifically central factorial LATE questions but rely on asymptotic randomization theory and first-stage strength."}}

topic: [v23/math/statistics] Analyze missing outcome data in randomized trials using principal stratification and sensitivity models. Compare likelihood-based, weighting-based, and Bayesian
full_prompt: Analyze missing outcome data in randomized trials using principal stratification and sensitivity models. Compare likelihood-based, weighting-based, and Bayesian approaches under nonignorable attrition.
completion:
rationale: To compare approaches for nonignorable attrition in randomized trials, I first identify recent work that explicitly combines principal stratification with MNAR/truncation mechanisms, then read candidate papers for assumptions, estimands, and sensitivity-model structure.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"principal stratification randomized trials missing not at random attrition sensitivity likelihood weighting Bayesian outcomes truncated by death OpenAlex","domain":"statistics","candidate_ids":["oa:W4399794334","oa:W4404988176"]}
```

[result] Found two directly relevant recent candidates:  
- `oa:W4399794334` — *Causal Inference with Outcomes Truncated by Death and Missing Not at Random*  
- `oa:W4404988176` — *Bounding causal effects with an unknown mixture of informative and non-informative missingness*

rationale: The first candidate is likely central because truncation by death and MNAR outcomes require principal strata, e.g. always-survivors, and sensitivity assumptions for unobserved outcomes.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W4399794334","sections":["abstract","methods","identification","sensitivity analysis"]}
```

[result] The paper formulates causal effects among latent principal strata defined by potential survival/truncation indicators. Missing outcomes among observed survivors are allowed to be MNAR. Identification requires assumptions linking observed-data distributions to latent principal strata, often through sensitivity parameters governing dependence between missingness and potential outcomes. Likelihood-based estimation is natural because the observed likelihood is a mixture over latent principal strata and missing outcomes.

rationale: The second candidate appears useful for contrasting point-identification models with partial-identification or bounding strategies when the amount of informative missingness is unknown.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W4404988176","sections":["abstract","model","bounds","discussion"]}
```

[result] The paper studies causal-effect bounds when missingness may be a mixture of informative and non-informative mechanisms. Rather than fully specifying the MNAR process, it introduces sensitivity parameters or mixture proportions controlling the fraction of missing outcomes generated by nonignorable mechanisms. This yields partial identification and robustness checks against misspecified missingness models.

full_prompt: Investigate causal effect estimation in panel data with staggered adoption and treatment effect heterogeneity. Compare synthetic control, difference-in-differences, and imputation estimators under violations of parallel trends.
completion:
Rationale: First identify core methodological references on staggered adoption, heterogeneous treatment effects, synthetic control, and imputation-based DiD, emphasizing assumptions and failure modes under nonparallel trends.

[result] simulated: Found central references:  
1. Goodman-Bacon, “Difference-in-Differences with Variation in Treatment Timing,” decomposes TWFE under staggered adoption and shows negative/contaminating weights.  
2. Callaway and Sant’Anna, “Difference-in-Differences with Multiple Time Periods,” estimates group-time ATT under no-anticipation and parallel trends.  
3. Sun and Abraham, “Estimating Dynamic Treatment Effects in Event Studies with Heterogeneous Treatment Effects,” diagnoses contamination in TWFE event studies.  
4. Borusyak, Jaravel, and Spiess, “Revisiting Event Study Designs: Robust and Efficient Estimation,” proposes imputation of untreated potential outcomes.  
5. Arkhangelsky et al., “Synthetic Difference-in-Differences,” combines synthetic-control weighting and DiD.  
6. Abadie, Diamond, and Hainmueller, “Synthetic Control Methods for Comparative Case Studies,” formalizes synthetic control under latent factor structures.

Key extraction:  
- TWFE with staggered adoption estimates a weighted average of all possible two-by-two DiD comparisons. With heterogeneous treatment effects, already-treated units can serve as controls for newly treated units, producing nonconvex or negative weights.  
- Modern DiD estimators target group-time effects \(ATT(g,t)=E[Y_t(1)-Y_t(0)\mid G=g]\), then aggregate with chosen weights.  
- Identification requires no anticipation and untreated potential-outcome parallel trends, e.g.  
  \[
  E[Y_t(0)-Y_{g-1}(0)\mid G=g]
  =
  E[Y_t(0)-Y_{g-1}(0)\mid C]
  \]
  for a suitable comparison set \(C\).  
- Imputation estimators fit an untreated-outcome model using untreated observations, predict \(\widehat Y_{it}(0)\) for treated observations, and estimate residual treatment effects \(Y_{it}-\widehat Y_{it}(0)\).  
- Under additive unit and time fixed effects, imputation and efficient DiD are closely related. Bias arises if untreated outcomes contain unmodeled group-specific trends or latent factors correlated with treatment timing.

Rationale: Read synthetic-control and synthetic-DiD work to assess whether weighting on pre-treatment outcomes can mitigate violations of unconditional parallel trends.

```action
    "Athey-Bayati-Doudchenko-Imbens-Khosravi-2021-matrix-completion"
  ],
  "focus": [
    "latent factor models",
    "pre-treatment fit",
    "synthetic control versus DiD",
    "nonparallel trends"
  ]
}
```

[result] simulated:  
Key extraction:  
- Synthetic control identifies counterfactual untreated paths by choosing donor weights \(w_j\) so that pre-treatment outcomes and covariates of treated units are approximated by a convex combination of controls.  
- It can handle violations of simple additive parallel trends when untreated outcomes follow a latent-factor model  
  \[
  Y_{it}(0)=\alpha_i+\lambda_t+\mu_i^\top f_t+\varepsilon_{it},
  \]
  provided pre-treatment outcomes are informative enough to balance \(\mu_i\).  
- Synthetic-DiD adds both unit weights and time weights, interpolating between SCM and DiD. It is more robust than DiD to level/trend imbalance and more stable than pure SCM when exact pre-treatment fit is impossible.  
- Matrix-completion/imputation methods generalize this logic by estimating low-rank untreated outcome structure, but rely on correct rank/regularization and sufficient untreated observations.

topic: [v23/math/statistics] Study selective inference after model selection in randomized experiments with many baseline covariates. Derive valid confidence intervals for treatment effects
full_prompt: Study selective inference after model selection in randomized experiments with many baseline covariates. Derive valid confidence intervals for treatment effects when covariate adjustment is chosen using data-adaptive procedures.
completion:
rationale: The supplied candidates appear unrelated to selective inference or randomized experiments, so I first screen them and then search for the relevant statistical literature on post-selection inference and regression adjustment in randomized trials. The target construction should exploit the known randomization distribution while conditioning on the covariate-adjustment rule’s selection event.
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["oa:W4414349998","oa:W2899258063"],"purpose":"Screen supplied OpenAlex candidates for relevance to selective inference after covariate-adjustment selection in randomized experiments."}
```
[result] simulated: Candidate [1] concerns livestock monitoring systems; candidate [2] concerns strategy training in language learning. Neither addresses randomized experiments, high-dimensional covariate adjustment, post-selection inference, or selective confidence intervals.

rationale: Since the candidates are off-topic, search for selective/post-selection inference and covariate adjustment in randomized experiments.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"selective inference post model selection randomized experiments covariate adjustment high dimensional baseline covariates treatment effect confidence intervals","limit":5}
```
[result] simulated: Relevant strands identified:  
1. Lee et al. “Exact post-selection inference, with application to the lasso” — polyhedral conditioning and truncated-normal pivots after affine selection events.  
2. Berk et al. “Valid post-selection inference” — simultaneous validity over possible selected linear models.  
3. Lin “Agnostic notes on regression adjustments to experimental data” — design-based validity of regression adjustment in randomized experiments.  
4. Bloniarz et al. “Lasso adjustments of treatment effect estimates in randomized experiments” — high-dimensional baseline adjustment under randomization.  
5. Fithian, Sun, and Taylor “Optimal inference after model selection” — conditional selective-inference framework.

rationale: Combine randomization-based treatment-effect inference with conditional selective inference. The key is to define the selected adjustment set as a measurable function of the observed data, condition on that selection event, and invert a selective pivot for the treatment contrast.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["Lee et al. 2016 exact post-selection inference","Lin 2013 regression adjustment randomized experiments","Bloniarz et al. 2016 lasso adjustments randomized experiments"],"purpose":"Extract technical ingredients for valid post-selection CIs after data-adaptive covariate adjustment."}
```
[result] simulated:  
- For Gaussian linear models with selection event \(\{Ay \le b\}\), any linear contrast \(\eta^\top y\) has a normal law truncated to an interval determined by \((A,b,\eta)\); inverting the selective pivot gives exact conditional intervals.  
- In randomized experiments, covariates are fixed and treatment assignment is random; regression adjustment can improve precision without requiring a correctly specified outcome model.  
- Lasso or other high-dimensional adjustment rules can be used for nuisance prediction, but naive post-selection standard errors ignore the selection event and can undercover.

Represent the resulting treatment estimator as a linear contrast eta_s^T Y. Conditional on X, Z, and A_s Y <= b_s, the contrast eta_s^T Y has a Gaussian or asymptotically Gaussian distribution truncated to an interval determined by A_s,b_s,eta_s. A valid selective confidence interval for tau is obtained by inverting the truncated-normal selective pivot, with variance estimated by a heteroskedasticity-robust or randomization-consistent estimator.",

topic: [v23/math/statistics] Study inference for regression discontinuity designs with multiple cutoffs and heterogeneous treatment effects. Develop bias-corrected estimators that account f
full_prompt: Study inference for regression discontinuity designs with multiple cutoffs and heterogeneous treatment effects. Develop bias-corrected estimators that account for cutoff-specific density discontinuities and bandwidth selection uncertainty.
completion:
rationale: I will first locate the core literatures to combine: multi-cutoff RD identification/aggregation, robust bias correction for local-polynomial RD, and inference that is valid after data-driven bandwidth choice.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"multiple cutoff regression discontinuity heterogeneous treatment effects robust bias correction bandwidth selection uncertainty density discontinuity","domain":"statistics econometrics","top_k":5}
```

[result] simulated:
1. OpenAlex W2097564891 — Calonico, Cattaneo & Titiunik (2014), “Robust Nonparametric Confidence Intervals for Regression-Discontinuity Designs,” Econometrica.
2. OpenAlex W2741057301 — Cattaneo, Keele, Titiunik & Vazquez-Bare (2016), “Interpreting Regression Discontinuity Designs with Multiple Cutoffs,” Journal of Politics.
3. OpenAlex W2962831682 — Cattaneo, Jansson & Ma (2020), “Simple Local Polynomial Density Estimators,” Journal of the American Statistical Association.
4. OpenAlex W2554506191 — Calonico, Cattaneo, Farrell & Titiunik (2017), “rdrobust: Software for Regression-Discontinuity Designs,” Stata Journal.
5. OpenAlex W2978349321 — Armstrong & Kolesár (2020), “Simple and Honest Confidence Intervals in Nonparametric Regression,” Quantitative Economics.

rationale: I will read the multi-cutoff RD paper to extract the estimand structure and how cutoff-specific effects are aggregated under heterogeneity.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"W2741057301","sections":["identification","estimation","heterogeneous effects","aggregation"]}
```

[result] simulated:
The paper defines cutoff-specific RD effects
\[
\tau_c=\lim_{x\downarrow c}E[Y\mid X=x,C=c]-\lim_{x\uparrow c}E[Y\mid X=x,C=c],
\]
and emphasizes that with multiple cutoffs the object is generally a collection \(\{\tau_c:c\in\mathcal C\}\), not a single homogeneous treatment effect. Aggregate effects require explicit weights, often depending on cutoff-group shares or target populations. Cross-cutoff extrapolation requires stronger comparability assumptions; without them, inference should remain cutoff-local or weight-local.

rationale: I will read robust bias correction and density-estimation references to determine how to modify local-polynomial RD when the running-variable density may jump differently at each cutoff and how to propagate bandwidth-selection error.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"W2097564891","sections":["bias correction","variance estimation","bandwidth choice","coverage"]}
```

[result] simulated:
Robust bias-corrected RD forms a local-polynomial estimator \(\hat\tau_p(h)\), estimates the leading smoothing bias using a higher-order local polynomial with bandwidth \(b\), and reports
\[
\hat\tau_{\mathrm{rbc}}=\hat\tau_p(h)-\hat B_{p+1}(b),
\]
with a standard error including variability of both the original estimator and the bias estimate. Conventional Wald intervals centered at \(\hat\tau_p(h)\) undercover when \(h\) is MSE-optimal; RBC intervals restore first-order validity under smooth conditional mean and regular density conditions, typically treating the selected bandwidth as fixed conditional on the data-generating process.

rationale: Density discontinuity at each cutoff can enter random-design local-polynomial expansions and variance normalization, so I will read the local polynomial density-estimation reference for side-specific density and derivative estimation.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"W2962831682","sections":["local polynomial density estimator","boundary estimation","asymptotic variance"]}
```

[result] simulated:
The paper gives boundary-adaptive local-polynomial estimators of \(f_X^{(v)}(c\pm)\) using empirical distribution function smoothing. These estimators can consistently estimate side-specific density levels and derivatives at a boundary point, supporting inference when the density has a jump:
\[
f_{c,+}=\lim_{x\downarrow c}f_{X\mid C=c}(x),\qquad
f_{c,-}=\lim_{x\uparrow c}f_{X\mid C=c}(x),
\]
with \(f_{c,+}\neq f_{c,-}\) allowed.

Use a robust bias-corrected estimator \\hat\\tau_{c,RBC}=\\hat\\tau_{c,p}(h_c)-\\hat B_{c,p+1}(b_c), where \\hat B_{c,p+1} is computed from higher-order one-sided fits. {"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Develop a design-based framework for two-stage randomized network experiments under partial interference. Partition units into independent clusters g=1,...,G, with interference allowed only inside clusters. In stage one, assign cluster g to a saturation or policy arm A_g∈𝒜 with known probability q_a. In stage two, assign unit i in cluster g treatment Z_i using a known degree-dependent rule p_i(a)=Pr(Z_i=1|A_g=a,D_i), for example p_i(a)=expit(α_a+β_a D_i), or fixed treatment counts within degree strata. Define an exposure mapping E_i=φ_i(Z_{N_i},D_{N_i}), such as treated-neighbor count, treated-neighbor fraction, or degree-normalized exposure category. Potential outcomes are Y_i(z,e), assuming partial interference and exposure-mapping sufficiency: Y_i(Z)=Y_i(Z_i,E_i). For each policy a, compute known exposure probabilities π_i^a(z,e)=Pr(Z_i=z,E_i=e|A_g=a,𝒢_g,D_g), using Bernoulli-product formulas or Poisson-binomial dynamic programming when neighbor assignments have unequal degree-dependent probabilities. [result] simulated: Relevant themes found:  
- Randomization inference by sharp-null testing and inversion for treatment effects.  
- Rank-score statistics such as Wilcoxon, van der Waerden, and general score functions, yielding distribution-free finite-sample tests under known assignment mechanisms.  
- QTE inference typically relies on asymptotic empirical-process methods or model-based variance estimation; exact/randomization-based QTE inference is less developed.  
- Stratified designs require conditioning on stratum-specific treatment counts or Bernoulli propensities.  
- Cluster-randomized designs require assignment at the cluster level, with outcome ranks handled either at the individual level with cluster-level randomization or through cluster summaries.  
- Unequal assignment probabilities require weighted rank statistics or Horvitz–Thompson/Hájek-style randomization distributions.

rationale: Propose a framework by combining sharp-null inversion with rank-score statistics indexed by quantile contrasts, while preserving the actual assignment law for stratification, clustering, and unequal probabilities.

```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Invert a family of finite-population randomization tests for sharp quantile-shift hypotheses. For a candidate quantile treatment effect parameter q, transform treated potential outcomes by subtracting q or, more generally, test H_q: Q_\tau(Y(1))-Q_\tau(Y(0))=q using imputed adjusted outcomes and rank-score statistics. For non-sharp QTE nulls, exactness requires conservative bounds, imputational assumptions, or optimization over compatible missing potential outcomes. Clustered designs may lose power when the number of clusters is small, and discrete outcomes can produce nonunique ranks and conservative p-values.","delta_genome":"Extend classical rank-based randomization tests from average or location-shift effects to QTE-oriented test inversion; define stratum-weighted and cluster-level rank-score statistics; incorporate unequal assignment probabilities through inverse-probability or design-calibrated scores; and provide algorithms for exact enumeration, dynamic programming within strata, or Monte Carlo sampling from the actual randomization scheme.","claim_genome":"The resulting procedure gives finite-sample valid, distribution-free confidence sets for QTEs under sharp or structurally sharpened quantile-shift hypotheses, and design-valid conservative inference for partially sharp QTE hypotheses. ```
```


[result] simulated: Found relevant strands: Anderson–Rubin inversion for weak-IV-robust confidence sets; overidentification/J-tests as restrictions on excess moments; Conley–Hansen–Rossi-style “plausibly exogenous” IV allowing bounded direct effects; weak-IV robust GMM/CLR methods; applications to Bartik/shift-share instruments where many instruments may be weak or imperfectly exogenous.

rationale: Read the supplied shift-share candidate to anchor the niche: Bartik/shift-share designs motivate many-instrument settings with interpretable shock-level exogeneity violations.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W2807611776"}
```

[result] simulated: The paper frames shift-share IV as exposure-weighted shocks and emphasizes identification assumptions at the shock level. It suggests that exogeneity can fail through correlated shocks or endogenous shares, motivating sensitivity analysis over direct violations. Many shocks also create overidentifying restrictions that can be used diagnostically, but conventional IV inference can be fragile under weak first stages.

rationale: Formulate a method by inverting a constrained Anderson–Rubin/GMM test. The key is to treat invalidity as a sensitivity parameter and use overidentifying restrictions to restrict—but not naively select—the admissible violation set.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"Anderson Rubin test GMM moment inequality sensitivity analysis invalid instruments overidentification projection confidence set"}
```

[result] simulated: Relevant technical template: define moments \(g_i(\beta,\gamma,\delta)=Z_i(Y_i-D_i\beta-X_i'\gamma)-\delta\), where \(\delta\) encodes instrument direct effects / moment violations. For fixed \((\beta,\eta)\), test whether there exists \(\gamma\) and \(\delta\in\Delta(\eta)\) such that the population moments vanish. Invert this test over \(\beta\), projecting over nuisance parameters and sensitivity radii. Critical values may be obtained from weak-IV-robust chi-square, chi-bar-square, or multiplier/bootstrap approximations under constrained GMM.

For a sensitivity radius eta, impose delta in Delta(eta), e.g. ||S^{-1}delta||_q <= eta or coordinatewise bounds |delta_j| <= eta_j. Define T_n(beta,eta)=inf_{gamma,delta in Delta(eta)} n * gbar(beta,gamma,delta)' Omega_hat^{-1} gbar(beta,gamma,delta). The eta-specific confidence set is C_eta={beta: T_n(beta,eta) <= c_{1-alpha}(eta)}. To incorporate overidentification restrictions, jointly invert the constrained moment test over (beta,eta) and then project: C={beta: exists eta in H_eta such that T_n(beta,eta) <= c_{1-alpha}^{sim}(eta)}. Equivalently, the smallest eta for which beta is not rejected is an overidentification-calibrated invalidity radius.",

{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Use a two-stage marketplace saturation design. Partition the platform into interference neighborhoods or markets m=1,...,M, assumed independent across markets. Then assign individual treatments A_im independently conditional on S_m and side. Let E_m(S_m) denote the endogenous equilibrium state, e.g. average transaction price, matching probability, queue length, congestion index, fill rate, or auction clearing statistic. Potential outcomes are Y_im(a,e) with market-level interference summarized by E_m. Estimate the reduced-form market dose-response μ(s)=E[\u0304Y_m(S_m=s)] by Horvitz-Thompson, Hájek, or augmented inverse-probability weighting, where \u0304Y_m is a weighted welfare, revenue, conversion, or surplus aggregate. Equilibrium multiplicity can make μ(s) set-valued unless the assignment selects a stable equilibrium selection rule.","delta_genome":"Define three estimands. First, the total equilibrium policy effect Ψ^tot(π,π0)=E[W_m(A^π,E^π)-W_m(A^π0,E^π0)], where W_m is market welfare, revenue, or surplus. Second, the direct-at-equilibrium-component effect Ψ^dir(a,a';e)=E[Y_im(a,e)-Y_im(a',e)], useful only as a controlled contrast at fixed equilibrium state e. Third, the equilibrium-mediated effect Ψ^med=Ψ^tot-Ψ^dir under a specified decomposition. Estimate Ψ^tot design-based from market-level saturation randomization; estimate mediated components with a structural or semiparametric equilibrium model for E_m(s,X_m) and outcome regression E[W_m|S_m,E_m,X_m], using cross-fitting and doubly robust scores when nuisance models are learned.","claim_genome":"A policy-relevant estimator for marketplace interference should target the market-level equilibrium dose-response rather than the treated-versus-control individual contrast. Report beta_hat, the corrected standard error, cluster leverages h_j=tr(H_j), effective df, and a sensitivity table using CR2 and CR3 corrections.",

A practical default is CR2-adjusted GEE with Satterthwaite df for the treatment contrast; CR3/Mancl-DeRouen should be used as a conservative sensitivity analysis. topic: [v23/math/statistics] Study causal discovery methods for time series with latent confounders and feedback loops. Develop statistical tests for directed dependence that control false
full_prompt: Study causal discovery methods for time series with latent confounders and feedback loops. Develop statistical tests for directed dependence that control false positives under autocorrelation and nonstationarity.
completion:
rationale: Search for time-series causal discovery work emphasizing lagged conditional independence, autocorrelation-aware testing, and limitations under hidden common causes/nonstationarity.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"causal discovery time series latent confounders feedback loops autocorrelation nonstationarity directed dependence tests PCMCI Granger causality","domain":"statistics"}
```

[result] simulated: Retrieved relevant lines of work: PCMCI/PCMCI+ for lagged causal discovery with autocorrelated time series; Granger-causal VAR and nonlinear conditional independence tests; surveys noting open problems with hidden confounders, feedback cycles, time-varying mechanisms, and false positive inflation under serial dependence.

rationale: Read candidate papers that directly cover PCMCI and broader time-series causal discovery assumptions.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["oa:W3007001386","oa:W4361193818"]}
```

[result] simulated:  
- `oa:W3007001386`: PCMCI estimates lagged causal parents by combining PC-style conditioning with momentary conditional independence tests. It reduces false positives from autocorrelation by conditioning on selected lagged parents, but standard validity assumes causal sufficiency or adequate observed conditioning and approximately stationary mechanisms.  
- `oa:W4361193818`: Survey distinguishes i.i.d. and time-series causal discovery, noting that temporal order helps orient lagged effects and allows feedback through cycles over time, but latent confounding, contemporaneous dependence, regime changes, and nonstationarity remain major sources of spurious directed edges.

rationale: Read broader symbolic/equation-discovery perspective to connect causal graph recovery with explicit time-varying structural equations.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W4388473546"}
```

[result] simulated: The paper emphasizes recovering causal relations and governing equations from observational data, but equation discovery is sensitive to omitted variables and distribution shifts. For time series, this motivates testing directed dependence on residual innovations after flexible nuisance modeling rather than interpreting raw predictive improvement as causality.

propose:
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Construct a locally stationary, factor-augmented directed-dependence test for multivariate time series. For variables X_i(t), define the null H0(i→j at lag set L): X_i,t−ℓ carries no directed information about X_j,t after conditioning on selected observed lagged parents, estimated latent common factors F_t, and local time-varying nuisance dynamics. Estimate nuisance regressions m_j,t(P_j,t) and m_i,t(P_i,t) by cross-fitted local VAR/GAM/ML smoothing, where P_j,t contains candidate parents excluding X_i lags plus factor scores from residual principal components. Form orthogonal innovations ε̂_j,t=X_j,t−m̂_j,t(P_j,t) and residualized predictors r̂_i,t−ℓ=X_i,t−ℓ−m̂_i,ℓ,t(P_j,t). Test directed dependence using a Neyman-orthogonal score S_ij=Σ_t Σ_{ℓ∈L} w_t r̂_i,t−ℓ ε̂_j,t, or a nonlinear version using residual HSIC/distance covariance on local windows.","niche_genome":"Designed for time-series causal discovery when ordinary PCMCI/Granger tests over-orient edges because of autocorrelation, slow distribution drift, latent common drivers, or feedback loops. Feedback is allowed as cycles across positive lags, e.g. X_i,t−1→X_j,t and X_j,t−1→X_i,t, while contemporaneous effects are left unoriented unless additional structural restrictions are supplied.","observation_genome":"Under β-mixing errors and local stationarity, raw lagged association has inflated type-I error because serial correlation reduces effective sample size and nonstationary trends induce spurious predictability. Residualizing both source lag and target innovation against local parents and latent factor estimates removes common low-rank confounding and nuisance autocorrelation to first order. A block wild bootstrap or stationary bootstrap applied to orthogonal score contributions preserves serial dependence, while max-T or Benjamini–Yekutieli correction controls familywise error or FDR over all ordered pairs and lags.","limitation_genome":"Identifiability is partial: latent confounders with idiosyncratic, non-low-rank effects may remain indistinguishable from direct lagged causation; instantaneous feedback cannot be oriented from observational sampling without stronger assumptions; severe regime switches require segmentation rather than smooth local stationarity; high-dimensional validity depends on sparse parent sets and consistent nuisance/factor estimation.","delta_genome":"Relative to standard PCMCI, add three safeguards: factor-augmented conditioning for hidden common causes, locally weighted/cross-fitted nuisance estimation for nonstationary dynamics, and bootstrap-calibrated orthogonal scores for autocorrelation-robust inference. The method reports directed lagged edges only when residual directed dependence survives latent-factor adjustment and multiplicity correction; otherwise it emits bidirected/ambiguous marks indicating possible hidden confounding.","claim_genome":"For each ordered pair (i,j), the proposed test targets the conditional moment H0: E[(X_i,t−ℓ−E[X_i,t−ℓ|C_t])(X_j,t−E[X_j,t|C_t])]=0 for all ℓ∈L, where C_t contains observed lagged parents, estimated latent factors, and local time index features. With consistent nuisance estimation, cross-fitting, and a dependence-preserving bootstrap, the resulting p-values are asymptotically valid under autocorrelated locally stationary errors, enabling controlled false positive rates for directed lagged-dependence discovery in the presence of feedback loops and approximate latent confounding."}}

topic: [v23/math/control_theory] Develop data-driven system identification methods that preserve passivity and dissipativity properties of the true plant. Use the identified model to synthesize
full_prompt: Develop data-driven system identification methods that preserve passivity and dissipativity properties of the true plant. Use the identified model to synthesize controllers with certified closed-loop stability margins.
completion:
rationale: The prompt asks for data-driven identification that preserves passivity/dissipativity and then uses the model for controller synthesis with certified stability margins. A natural formal framework is port-Hamiltonian / dissipativity theory, because passivity is encoded structurally through storage functions, skew-symmetric interconnection, positive semidefinite dissipation, and supply rates.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"data-driven system identification passivity dissipativity preserving port-Hamiltonian controller stability margins","domain":"control_theory"}
```

[result] simulated: Relevant themes found: dissipativity-constrained subspace identification, passive system identification via positive-real constraints, port-Hamiltonian identification from data, Willems-fundamental-lemma data-driven dissipativity tests, and passivity-based robust controller synthesis. Candidate [2] `oa:W2858992092` is relevant as background for port-Hamiltonian structure and passivity.

rationale: Read the port-Hamiltonian candidate to extract structural constraints suitable for identification.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W2858992092"}
```

[result] simulated: The paper develops port-Hamiltonian systems on graphs/complexes, emphasizing Dirac interconnection structures, Hamiltonian storage, dissipation operators, and port variables. Core implication: if the learned model preserves the algebraic port-Hamiltonian structure  
\[
\dot x=(J-R)\nabla H(x)+Gu,\qquad y=G^\top \nabla H(x),
\]
with \(J=-J^\top\), \(R\succeq 0\), and \(H(x)\ge 0\), then the model is passive:
\[
\dot H \le y^\top u.
\]

rationale: Formulate a concrete identification-and-control mechanism combining constrained regression with dissipativity LMIs and robust passivity margins.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"dissipativity constrained system identification LMI positive real lemma robust passivity margin control"}
```

[result] simulated: Methods cluster around: (i) estimating state-space matrices under KYP/positive-real LMI constraints; (ii) fitting storage matrices \(P\succ 0\) jointly with system matrices; (iii) using passivity indices \((\nu,\rho)\) to certify input/output strict passivity; and (iv) synthesizing controllers by passivity theorem or LMI-based \(H_\infty\)/dissipativity constraints.

propose:
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Develop a constrained data-driven identification pipeline in which the model class is either port-Hamiltonian or dissipativity-certified state-space. For linear systems, identify matrices (A,B,C,D) and a storage matrix P=P^T≻0 by solving a regularized prediction-error problem subject to the KYP dissipativity LMI: [A^T P+PA  PB-C^T S  C^T Q; B^T P-S^T C  -(D^T S+S^T D)+R-D^T QD  -D^T Q; QC  -QD  Q] ⪯ 0 for a quadratic supply rate w(u,y)=y^T Q y+2y^T S u+u^T R u. For passivity, specialize to w(u,y)=y^T u or enforce positive-real constraints. For nonlinear systems, parameterize a port-Hamiltonian model xdot=(J_theta(x)-R_theta(x))∇H_theta(x)+G_theta(x)u, y=G_theta(x)^T∇H_theta(x), with J_theta=-J_theta^T, R_theta⪰0, H_theta bounded below, thereby making passivity structural rather than post hoc.","niche_genome":"Passivity- and dissipativity-preserving system identification for safety-critical control applications where black-box predictive accuracy alone is insufficient: power electronics, robotics, vehicle energy systems, networked physical systems, and port-Hamiltonian plants.","observation_genome":"Unconstrained data-driven identification can fit input-output trajectories accurately while violating positive-realness or dissipativity, leading to controllers whose nominal closed-loop simulations appear stable but lack certifiable margins. Enforcing storage-function or port-Hamiltonian constraints during identification converts physical energy inequalities into convex LMIs in the linear case or structured semidefinite/PSD parameterizations in the nonlinear case.","limitation_genome":"The approach may be conservative because the selected storage function class, e.g. quadratic P or restricted Hamiltonian H_theta, may not capture the true plant's dissipativity certificate. Nonlinear port-Hamiltonian identification can be nonconvex, requires sufficiently exciting data, and may suffer from bias if numerical differentiation or unmeasured states are handled poorly. Certified margins are only as reliable as the noise model, excitation richness, and uncertainty bounds used during identification.","delta_genome":"Compared with standard subspace or neural state-space identification, add hard dissipativity constraints, uncertainty sets around the identified model, and controller synthesis that explicitly consumes the identified passivity indices. The controller is synthesized to preserve a closed-loop dissipation inequality with margin, e.g. by requiring the learned plant to be output strictly passive with index rho>0 and the controller to be input strictly passive with compatible index, or by solving a robust KYP/LMI condition over the identification uncertainty set.","claim_genome":"A model identified under KYP or port-Hamiltonian structural constraints admits an explicit storage function V(x)=x^T P x or H_theta(x), hence satisfies a certified dissipativity inequality on the training-valid operating domain. If the learned plant is certified passive with quantified passivity indices and the controller is synthesized to satisfy the complementary dissipativity inequality under the same uncertainty set, then the closed loop is Lyapunov stable; with strict input/output passivity margins, it is asymptotically stable and possesses computable gain/phase or dissipativity robustness margins."}}

For binary treatment and centered weighted least squares, the local information contribution is approximately I_t p_t(H_t)(1-p_t(H_t)) f_t(H_t)f_t(H_t)^T / σ_t^2, so design precision is maximized by allocating randomization variance where availability is high, residual variance is low, and covariate features are informative for regime decisions.","niche_genome":"Micro-randomized trials for mobile health, behavioral interventions, just-in-time adaptive interventions, and other longitudinal settings where repeated randomization is possible but participant burden and ethical exposure limits constrain experimentation.","observation_genome":"Use a constrained optimal design problem: minimize tr(V(β_hat)) or -log det information for the target causal/regime parameters, subject to positivity ε ≤ p_t(a|H_t) ≤ 1-ε, expected burden constraints sum_t E[I_t p_t(1|H_t)c_t(H_t)] ≤ B, per-window exposure caps, and ethical constraints limiting assignment to actions with unacceptable predicted risk. topic: [v23/math/control_theory] Develop a nonlinear model predictive control scheme for input-constrained systems with state-dependent uncertainty and prove recursive feasibility under tube-ba
full_prompt: Develop a nonlinear model predictive control scheme for input-constrained systems with state-dependent uncertainty and prove recursive feasibility under tube-based tightening. Compare performance against robust MPC with polytopic uncertainty sets on a benchmark underactuated system.
completion:
rationale: The task needs a tube-based nonlinear MPC construction, a recursive-feasibility argument, and an experimental comparison baseline. I first search for the relevant NMPC tube/robust MPC ingredients and then read the most relevant proof patterns.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"tube-based nonlinear model predictive control recursive feasibility state dependent uncertainty input constraints robust MPC polytopic uncertainty benchmark underactuated system","domain":"control_theory","top_k":5}
```

[result] simulated: Found relevant threads: robust/tube MPC with constraint tightening; nonlinear MPC with incremental stability assumptions; robust MPC with polytopic uncertainty sets; underactuated benchmarks such as cart-pole, acrobot, and pendubot. Key reusable proof ingredients: nominal terminal set, local ancillary feedback, Pontryagin-difference tightening, disturbance-propagation recursion, and shifted optimal sequence.

rationale: I read the proof templates for recursive feasibility and the comparison baseline so the proposed scheme can be technically precise and not merely algorithmic.

```action
{"tool":"read","sources":["tube-based MPC recursive feasibility via tightened constraints","nonlinear robust MPC with incremental stabilizability","robust MPC for polytopic uncertain systems","cart-pole/acrobot NMPC benchmarks"],"extract":["assumptions","tightening law","recursive feasibility proof","baseline formulation"]}
```

[result] simulated: Extracted structure: For discrete-time nonlinear dynamics x⁺=f(x,u)+w with w∈W(x,u), use nominal z⁺=f(z,v), actual input u=v+K(x-z). If tube error e=x-z satisfies e⁺∈A_K(z,v)e⊕W(z,v)⊕higher-order remainder, define cross-sections E_k recursively and impose z_k∈X⊖E_k, v_k∈U⊖K E_k. Recursive feasibility follows by shifting the optimal nominal sequence, appending terminal controller κ_f, and showing the shifted tube sequence remains admissible. Polytopic robust MPC baseline usually uses a fixed W=conv{w¹,…,w^q} or vertex models and therefore tightens uniformly, often conservatively when uncertainty is state-dependent.

rationale: The key design choice is to exploit state-dependent uncertainty W(x,u) instead of replacing it by a global polytope. This should reduce tightening near benign regions while preserving worst-case guarantees through local tube propagation.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"cart pole nonlinear MPC robust MPC input constraints underactuated benchmark uncertainty comparison","domain":"control_theory","top_k":3}
```

[result] simulated: Cart-pole is the most suitable benchmark because it is underactuated, nonlinear, input-limited, and has common constraints on cart position and force. State-dependent uncertainty can be modeled as friction and mass/inertia mismatch entering through configuration-dependent terms, e.g. W(x,u)=B_w(x)D with ||D||≤ρ(x,u), where ρ increases with velocity and pole angle.

The optimizer uses the nominal model z_{k+1}=f(z_k,v_k), cost Σℓ(z_k,v_k)+V_f(z_N), and applies u_t=v_0^*+K_t(x_t-z_0^*). State-dependent uncertainty is over-approximated locally by W_k:=G(z_k,v_k)D(z_k,v_k)⊕L_kE_k, where E_k is the current tube section and L_k bounds the nonlinear remainder. Tube sections satisfy E_0={0}, E_{k+1}=A_{K,k}E_k⊕W_k, with A_{K,k}=A_k+B_kK_t. Tighten constraints by z_k∈X⊖E_k and v_k∈U⊖K_tE_k, and impose a terminal condition z_N∈X_f with κ_f satisfying f(z,κ_f(z))∈X_f and local tightened invariance.","niche_genome":"Input-constrained nonlinear systems whose uncertainty magnitude or direction depends on state, such as underactuated mechanical systems with configuration-dependent inertia error, velocity-dependent friction, or angle-dependent unmodeled coupling. The method is aimed at cases where a global polytopic uncertainty set is safe but excessively conservative.","observation_genome":"A fixed polytopic robust MPC baseline replaces W(x,u) by W_global=conv⋃_{(x,u)∈X×U}W(x,u), causing identical tightening in low- and high-uncertainty regions. The proposed NMPC instead propagates W_k along the predicted trajectory, so the tube is small near upright/low-velocity cart-pole motions and expands only where the local uncertainty bound is large.","limitation_genome":"Recursive feasibility requires computable outer approximations E_k, compact X,U, bounded state-dependent uncertainty, and local incremental stabilizability under K_t. If local linearization/remainder bounds are too loose, the method may lose its advantage over polytopic robust MPC. Stability additionally requires a compatible terminal Lyapunov decrease condition, not just feasibility.","delta_genome":"Compared with standard robust MPC using a single polytopic uncertainty set, the proposed scheme replaces uniform worst-case tightening with trajectory-dependent tube tightening. Expected delta: larger feasible set and lower cost because the controller does not reserve worst-case uncertainty margins at all predicted states.","claim_genome":"Recursive feasibility proof: Suppose the NMPC problem is feasible at time t with optimal nominal sequence {z_k^*,v_k^*}_{k=0}^{N-1}, tube sections E_k^*, and terminal z_N^*∈X_f. At time t+1 choose the shifted candidate \\tilde z_k=z_{k+1}^*, \\tilde v_k=v_{k+1}^* for k=0,…,N-2, and append \\tilde v_{N-1}=κ_f(z_N^*). Define shifted tube sections by \\tilde E_0={x_{t+1}-z_1^*}⊆E_1^* and \\tilde E_{k+1}=A_{K,k}\\tilde E_k⊕\\tilde W_k. Since the previous plan satisfied z_{k+1}^*∈X⊖E_{k+1}^* and v_{k+1}^*∈U⊖K_tE_{k+1}^*, and the propagated shifted tubes are contained in the corresponding admissible outer approximations, all tightened constraints remain satisfied for k=0,…,N-2. Terminal robust invariance of X_f under κ_f yields \\tilde z_N∈X_f with tightened terminal admissibility. Hence a feasible candidate exists at t+1 for every admissible uncertainty realization, proving recursive feasibility."}}

topic: [v23/math/control_theory] Study optimal control of stochastic differential equations with multiplicative noise and state constraints. Derive Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman conditions and compar
full_prompt: Study optimal control of stochastic differential equations with multiplicative noise and state constraints. Derive Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman conditions and compare numerical solutions using stochastic MPC and policy iteration.
completion:
rationale: I will ground the study in dynamic programming for controlled diffusions, using the supplied decision-making reference as algorithmic scaffolding, then formulate the constrained HJB and a numerical comparison between stochastic MPC and policy iteration.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"stochastic optimal control SDE multiplicative noise state constraints Hamilton Jacobi Bellman viscosity solution stochastic MPC policy iteration","domain":"control_theory","candidate_paper_ids":["oa:W2954742730"],"k":5}
```  
[result] simulated: Search identifies three relevant technical clusters: (i) controlled Itô diffusions with generator \(\mathcal L^u V=f(x,u)\cdot \nabla V+\frac12\operatorname{tr}[\sigma\sigma^\top(x,u)\nabla^2 V]\), where multiplicative noise enters the second-order term; (ii) state-constrained HJB theory using viscosity solutions with viability/tangency or state-constraint boundary conditions; (iii) numerical dynamic programming/policy iteration and stochastic MPC methods for approximating constrained stochastic optimal control.

rationale: The candidate text is not specifically about continuous-time constrained diffusions, but it is relevant for Bellman optimality, value iteration, policy iteration, and approximate dynamic programming comparisons.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W2954742730","sections":["dynamic programming","Markov decision processes","policy iteration","approximate dynamic programming"]}
```  
[result] simulated: The source emphasizes Bellman fixed-point equations, policy evaluation/improvement, value iteration, and finite-state approximations. These transfer to controlled diffusions after spatial discretization or Markov chain approximation: policy iteration alternates solving a linear elliptic/parabolic PDE for a fixed feedback and minimizing the Hamiltonian pointwise; MPC instead repeatedly solves finite-horizon constrained stochastic programs and applies the first control.

propose:
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Model the controlled state as an Itô diffusion on a constrained domain K: dX_t=f(X_t,u_t)dt+Sigma(X_t,u_t)dW_t, X_t in K, u_t in U, with multiplicative noise through state/control-dependent diffusion matrix a(x,u)=Sigma(x,u)Sigma(x,u)^T. For infinite-horizon discounted cost J^u(x)=E_x[int_0^infty exp(-rho t) ell(X_t,u_t)dt], the value V satisfies the constrained HJB condition rho V(x)=inf_{u in U_K(x)}{ell(x,u)+f(x,u)·grad V(x)+1/2 tr[a(x,u) Hess V(x)]} for x in int K, interpreted in the viscosity sense. The admissible set U_K(x) enforces stochastic viability at the boundary; formally, admissible controls must prevent outward probability flux or satisfy an appropriate state-constraint boundary condition rather than a classical Dirichlet condition.","niche_genome":"Continuous-time stochastic optimal control with multiplicative noise, hard or probabilistic state constraints, and numerical solution via stochastic model predictive control versus dynamic-programming policy iteration.","observation_genome":"Multiplicative noise changes the HJB not only quantitatively but structurally, because the minimizing control may enter the second-order elliptic operator through a(x,u). Thus policy improvement requires minimizing a Hamiltonian containing Hess V, while stochastic MPC must propagate covariance or scenario dynamics under state-dependent noise. Policy iteration is closer to the HJB optimum on low-dimensional grids but suffers from curse-of-dimensionality and delicate boundary treatment. Stochastic MPC scales better to nonlinear constrained systems and can handle chance constraints naturally, but receding-horizon truncation and sampling/collocation error generally produce suboptimal feedback relative to the stationary HJB solution.","limitation_genome":"Classical smooth HJB derivations require V in C^2 and uniform ellipticity, assumptions often violated by degenerate multiplicative noise, nonsmooth constraints, and control-dependent diffusion. For hard state constraints, feasibility may fail unless viability conditions hold on partial K. Numerical comparison must distinguish discretization error, sampling error, horizon truncation, and constraint-tightening conservatism.","delta_genome":"Derive the HJB from the dynamic programming principle for constrained controlled diffusions, then implement two approximations on the same benchmark: (1) semi-Lagrangian or finite-difference policy iteration with monotone discretization of the controlled second-order operator and viscosity-consistent state-constraint boundary conditions; (2) stochastic MPC using scenario trees, polynomial chaos, or unscented covariance propagation with chance-constraint tightening. Compare cost, violation probability, feedback regularity, runtime, and sensitivity to noise amplitude.","claim_genome":"For low-dimensional constrained SDEs with moderate multiplicative noise, monotone policy iteration should converge to the viscosity solution of the constrained HJB and provide the most accurate stationary feedback. For higher-dimensional or strongly nonlinear systems, stochastic MPC should yield more computationally tractable controllers with explicit constraint handling, but its achieved cost will depend on horizon length, scenario budget, and conservative constraint tightening; hence it should be evaluated as an approximate receding-horizon realization of the HJB-optimal feedback, not as an exact dynamic-programming solution."}}

topic: [v23/math/control_theory] Formulate robust adaptive control laws for nonlinear systems with unmatched uncertainties and time-varying parameters. Prove boundedness and convergence propert
full_prompt: Formulate robust adaptive control laws for nonlinear systems with unmatched uncertainties and time-varying parameters. Prove boundedness and convergence properties using composite adaptation and dynamic regressor extension techniques.
completion:

Rationale: I will anchor the formulation in two established threads—composite adaptive control and DREM-style parameter estimation—then synthesize a robust nonlinear control law for systems with unmatched, time-varying parametric uncertainty.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"composite adaptive control nonlinear systems dynamic regressor extension mixing DREM unmatched uncertainties time varying parameters"}
```

[result] Simulated search result: Relevant sources include composite adaptive control for nonlinear systems, prediction-error-based adaptation, dynamic regressor extension and mixing for finite/exponential parameter estimation under interval excitation, and robust adaptive/backstepping methods for unmatched uncertainties.

```action
{"tool":"read","query":"dynamic regressor extension and mixing composite adaptation nonlinear adaptive control unmatched uncertainty"}
```

[result] Simulated read result: DREM converts vector regressions \(y=\phi^	op	heta\) into scalar regressions \(Y_i=\Delta 	heta_i+\varepsilon_i\) using stable filters and adjugate/determinant mixing. Composite adaptation combines tracking-error adaptation with prediction-error adaptation. For nonlinear systems with unmatched uncertainty, robust control typically requires a stabilizing nominal CLF/backstepping design plus damping or ISS terms to dominate residual unmatched perturbations.

propose:
```json

  "tool": "propose",
  "gene_genome": {
    "mechanism_genome": "Consider the nonlinear uncertain system
\[
\dot x=f_0(x)+g(x)u+F_m(x)\theta_m(t)+F_u(x)\theta_u(t)+d(x,t),\qquad x\in\mathbb R^n,
\]
where \(g(x)u\) is the matched input channel, \(F_m(x)\theta_m\) is matched or feedback-linearizable uncertainty, \(F_u(x)\theta_u\) is unmatched uncertainty, and \(d\) is bounded. Let \(V(x)\) be a nominal control Lyapunov function satisfying, for a nominal stabilizer \(u_0(x)\),
\[
\dot V \le -\alpha_1(\|x\|)+L_gV(x)(u-u_0)+L_{F_m}V(x)\tilde\theta_m+L_{F_u}V(x)\tilde\theta_u+\rho(x)\bar d .
\]
Use the robust adaptive control law
\[
u=u_0(x)-K(x)L_gV(x)^\top-\hat\theta_m^\top \psi_m(x)-\sigma_r(x),
\]
where \(K(x)=K(x)^\top>0\), \(\psi_m\) cancels the matched regressor in the CLF derivative, and \(\sigma_r(x)\) is a smooth robust damping term satisfying
\[
L_gV\,\sigma_r \ge \frac{1}{2\eta}\|L_{F_u}V\,\tilde\theta_u\|^2+\frac{1}{2\eta}\rho^2(x)\bar d^2
\]
for design margin \(\eta>0\). Parameter estimates are updated by a composite DREM law. From filtered regressions
\[
y_f(t)=\Phi_f(t)\theta(t)+\varepsilon_f(t),
\]
construct the DREM extension
\[
Y(t)=\operatorname{adj}\{\Phi_e(t)\}y_e(t),\qquad \Delta(t)=\det\{\Phi_e(t)\},
\]
so that componentwise
\[
Y_i(t)=\Delta(t)\theta_i(t)+\bar\varepsilon_i(t).
\]
The adaptive law is
\[
\dot{\hat\theta}_i
= -\Gamma_i \omega_i(x)e_V(x)
  -\gamma_i \Delta(t)\bigl(\Delta(t)\hat\theta_i-Y_i(t)\bigr)
  -\ell_i \hat\theta_i,
\]
where \(e_V=L_\theta V\) is the tracking-error regressor, \(\omega_i(x)\) is the corresponding CLF sensitivity, \(\Gamma_i,\gamma_i>0\), and \(\ell_i>0\) is a leakage term used for slowly time-varying parameters.",
    "niche_genome": "The construction targets nonlinear systems whose uncertainties are not purely matched and whose unknown parameters may drift slowly in time. Unlike classical certainty-equivalence adaptive control, the proposed design separates stabilization from identification: the CLF/backstepping part guarantees robust ISS-type stabilization despite unmatched uncertainty, while the composite DREM estimator improves parameter convergence under interval excitation rather than requiring full persistent excitation.",
    "observation_genome": "Let \(\tilde\theta=\hat\theta-\theta(t)\) and define
\[
\mathcal V(x,\tilde\theta)=V(x)+\frac12\tilde\theta^\top\Gamma^{-1}\tilde\theta.
\]

\[
\dot{\mathcal V}
\le -\alpha_1(\|x\|)-\|L_gV\|_{K}^{2}
   -\sum_i \gamma_i\Delta^2\tilde\theta_i^2
   +c_1\|\tilde\theta_u\|^2+c_2\|\dot\theta(t)\|^2+c_3\|\bar\varepsilon\|^2,
\]
after completing squares and using the robust damping term to dominate unmatched and disturbance contributions. If \(\theta(t)\) is constant, \(d=0\), and the DREM determinant satisfies the interval-excitation condition
\[
\int_t^{t+T}\Delta^2(\tau)d\tau\ge \mu>0,
\]
then \(\tilde\theta_i(t)\to0\) exponentially for all identifiable components and \(x(t)\to0\). If \(\theta(t)\) is slowly time-varying with \(\|\dot\theta\|\le \nu\), then all closed-loop signals are uniformly ultimately bounded with ultimate radius \(O(\nu+\|d\|_\infty+\|\bar\varepsilon\|_\infty)\).",

    "delta_genome": "The main technical increment is the coupling of a robust CLF/backstepping stabilizer for unmatched nonlinear uncertainty with a composite DREM estimator. Classical adaptive laws use only tracking-error feedback and usually require persistent excitation for parameter convergence. Here, prediction-error channels generated by dynamic regressor extension yield scalar adaptation laws with weaker excitation requirements and improved transients, while leakage/modification terms preserve boundedness under time-varying parameters.",
```

topic: [v23/math/control_theory] Investigate Koopman operator-based control for nonlinear systems with control-affine dynamics. Construct finite-dimensional lifted representations and quantify
full_prompt: Investigate Koopman operator-based control for nonlinear systems with control-affine dynamics. Construct finite-dimensional lifted representations and quantify the impact of basis truncation on closed-loop stability.
completion:
rationale: Koopman-control work often uses finite dictionaries to obtain linear predictors, but control-affine nonlinear systems naturally induce a bilinear lifted generator. I first search for Koopman-control references and stability/truncation gaps.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"Koopman operator control-affine nonlinear systems EDMD bilinear lifted model closed-loop stability truncation error","domain":"control_theory","candidates":["oa:W3173624651","oa:W4367056604","oa:W4382981733"]}
```

[result] simulated: Search identifies Koopman-based modeling/control as central for nonlinear robotic systems, with finite-dimensional dictionaries used for MPC/LQR-style synthesis. Candidate `oa:W4382981733` is directly relevant as a Koopman-control survey/application paper for soft robotics. Candidate `oa:W3173624651` is relevant to reduced-order closure/truncation effects, though not Koopman-specific. Stability guarantees under dictionary truncation appear underdeveloped compared with prediction-error studies.

rationale: Read the Koopman-control candidate to extract the standard lifted representation and its limitations.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W4382981733","focus":["finite-dimensional Koopman lifting","control synthesis","limitations of truncated dictionaries"]}
```

[result] simulated: The paper discusses constructing lifted coordinates from observable dictionaries and fitting finite-dimensional predictors for nonlinear soft-robot dynamics. Control is typically performed using the learned lifted model inside MPC or linear-control templates. The main practical limitation is that the chosen finite dictionary is not Koopman-invariant, so the learned operator contains approximation error; closed-loop stability under this truncation is not generally certified.

rationale: Read the reduced-order closure candidate to connect basis truncation with unresolved-mode forcing and stability degradation.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W3173624651","focus":["basis truncation","closure terms","reduced-order model stability"]}
```

[result] simulated: The paper frames reduced-order truncation as inducing missing closure terms from unresolved modes. Closure errors can act as structured disturbances on the retained coordinates, affecting long-time prediction and stability. This suggests treating Koopman dictionary truncation residuals as perturbations in the lifted closed-loop dynamics.

propose:
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"For a control-affine nonlinear system \\(\\dot x=f(x)+\\sum_{i=1}^m g_i(x)u_i\\), choose observables \\(\\psi_N:X\\to\\mathbb R^N\\) and define \\(z=\\psi_N(x)\\). The Koopman generator satisfies \\(\\frac{d}{dt}\\psi=L_f\\psi+\\sum_i u_i L_{g_i}\\psi\\). Projecting onto \\(\\mathrm{span}\\{\\psi_1,\\ldots,\\psi_N\\}\\) yields the finite lifted bilinear model \\(\\dot z=A_Nz+\\sum_i u_i B_{i,N}z+r_N(x,u)\\), with reconstruction \\(x\\approx C_Nz\\). The truncation residual \\(r_N\\) is the component of the generator outside the retained dictionary and can be bounded on a compact operating set.","niche_genome":"Koopman operator-based control for smooth control-affine nonlinear systems on compact invariant sets, especially where finite-dimensional EDMD/Galerkin dictionaries are used for LQR, CLF, or MPC synthesis but the dictionary is not exactly Koopman-invariant.","observation_genome":"Existing Koopman-control implementations commonly stabilize or optimize the learned finite lifted model while treating dictionary truncation mainly as prediction error. Reduced-order modeling theory suggests that omitted Koopman modes act as closure forcing on the retained lifted coordinates, so closed-loop stability should be analyzed as robustness of the nominal lifted controller to a structured generator residual.","limitation_genome":"Exact finite-dimensional Koopman-invariant subspaces rarely exist for nonlinear control-affine systems. The lifted model is generally bilinear in \\((z,u)\\), not purely linear, unless inputs are appended or locally linearized. Stability certificates depend on uniform residual bounds over the closed-loop reachable set, which may be difficult to estimate from finite noisy data.","delta_genome":"Quantify truncation impact by computing or estimating \\(\\epsilon_N=\\sup_{x\\in K,u\\in U}\\|r_N(x,u)\\|\\). If a nominal lifted feedback \\(u=\\kappa_N(z)\\) gives closed-loop matrix/vector field \\(F_N(z)\\) with Lyapunov certificate \\(\\nabla V(z)F_N(z)\\le -\\alpha\\|z\\|^2\\), then the true truncated closed loop satisfies \\(\\dot V\\le -\\alpha\\|z\\|^2+\\|\nabla V(z)\\|\\epsilon_N\\). For quadratic \\(V=z^TPz\\), this gives \\(\\dot V\\le -\\alpha\\|z\\|^2+2\\|P\\|\\epsilon_N\\|z\\|\\), implying practical stability with ultimate radius \\(O(\\|P\\|\\epsilon_N/\\alpha)\\), and asymptotic stability when the residual is sufficiently small relative to the Lyapunov margin and vanishes at the equilibrium.","claim_genome":"A finite-dimensional Koopman controller for a control-affine system should be certified by a residual-aware Lyapunov inequality: nominal lifted stability margin minus a computable Koopman truncation residual. The resulting closed-loop degradation scales linearly with the basis-truncation residual and inversely with the nominal Lyapunov decay margin, yielding an explicit stability-radius tradeoff for dictionary size selection."}}
