topic: [v23/math/theoretical_cs] Investigate whether fine-grained lower bounds for dynamic reachability in directed graphs can be strengthened under variants of the Online Matrix-Vector multipl
full_prompt: Investigate whether fine-grained lower bounds for dynamic reachability in directed graphs can be strengthened under variants of the Online Matrix-Vector multiplication conjecture. Focus on update/query tradeoffs when the graph has bounded treewidth or bounded feedback vertex set number.
completion:
rationale: I will first locate the canonical OMv-based dynamic reachability lower-bound construction, then check whether its hard instances have structural parameters compatible with bounded treewidth or bounded feedback vertex set. The key technical issue is whether the dense matrix gadget can be encoded while keeping the underlying undirected structure sparse/low-width, or whether only a directed acyclicity parameter remains small.
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"OMv conjecture dynamic s-t reachability lower bound directed graph treewidth feedback vertex set"}
```

[result] simulated:
- Henzinger, Krinninger, Nanongkai, Saranurak, “Unifying and Strengthening Hardness for Dynamic Problems via the Online Matrix-Vector Multiplication Conjecture,” STOC 2015 / SICOMP 2020. Establishes OMv-based lower bounds for partially dynamic and fully dynamic reachability/transitive closure.
- Abboud and Vassilevska Williams, “Popular Conjectures Imply Strong Lower Bounds for Dynamic Problems,” FOCS 2014. Related dynamic lower bounds from APSP/3SUM-type conjectures.
- Several algorithmic papers on dynamic graph problems parameterized by treewidth or feedback vertex set, but no direct standard OMv lower bound for dynamic reachability on constant-treewidth directed graphs.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper":"Henzinger-Krinninger-Nanongkai-Saranurak OMv dynamic reachability lower bound"}
```

[result] simulated:
The standard OMv-to-dynamic-reachability reduction uses a tripartite directed graph with layers \(L,R\) and terminals \(s,t\). The Boolean matrix \(M\in\{0,1\}^{n\times n}\) is encoded as static edges \(u_i\to v_j\) whenever \(M_{ij}=1\). Each online vector pair is encoded by updating edges incident to \(s\) and \(t\), e.g. \(s\to u_i\) for active left coordinates and \(v_j\to t\) for active right coordinates. A query asks whether \(s\leadsto t\), which is equivalent to \(u^\top M v=1\).

Structural observation:
- The graph is a DAG, so its directed feedback vertex set number is \(0\).
- Its underlying undirected graph contains an arbitrary bipartite graph realizing \(M\), so its treewidth and undirected feedback vertex set number can be \(\Omega(n)\).
- Therefore the known construction already gives hardness for directed-acyclic graphs, but not for bounded underlying treewidth or bounded undirected feedback vertex set.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"dynamic reachability bounded treewidth directed graphs update query time"}
```

[result] simulated:
- Dynamic reachability on graph classes of bounded treewidth is usually approached by maintaining summaries over a tree decomposition.
- For fixed treewidth \(k\), reachability between boundary vertices of bags can be represented by finite transitive-closure summaries of size \(2^{O(k^2)}\), yielding polylogarithmic or logarithmic update/query bounds under standard decomposition assumptions.
- These algorithmic results suggest that unconditional OMv-style \(n^{1-o(1)}\) lower bounds are unlikely for constant treewidth unless the OMv variant encodes matrix information outside the graph topology, e.g. in weights, labels, or batched/adaptive update sequences.

propose:
```json
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "gene_genome": {
    "mechanism_genome": "The standard OMv reduction for dynamic directed s-t reachability encodes the Boolean matrix M as a static bipartite layer of directed edges U -> V, and encodes online vectors by updates to terminal-incidence edges s -> U and V -> t. The query s \\leadsto t computes whether there exist i,j with u_i=1, M_{ij}=1, and v_j=1. This construction is acyclic, so it has directed feedback vertex set number 0, but its underlying undirected graph has arbitrary bipartite structure and hence can have treewidth and undirected feedback vertex set number \\Omega(n).",
    "niche_genome": "Fine-grained lower bounds for dynamic reachability under structural graph restrictions, specifically bounded treewidth and bounded feedback vertex set number. The meaningful distinction is between directed feedback vertex set, where the OMv construction is already maximally strong because the hard instances are DAGs, and undirected treewidth / undirected feedback vertex set, where the dense matrix gadget destroys bounded-width structure.",
    "observation_genome": "For constant treewidth k, reachability information across a bag boundary can be summarized by a relation on O(k) boundary vertices, with state space roughly 2^{O(k^2)}. Dynamic updates can be propagated through a tree decomposition using local recomputation and balanced dynamic-tree techniques. Thus the natural matrix-as-bipartite-graph OMv gadget is incompatible with constant underlying treewidth, and there is an algorithmic barrier to proving standard OMv lower bounds with polynomial update/query tradeoffs on fixed-k treewidth graphs.",
    "limitation_genome": "A strengthening under ordinary OMv is plausible for directed feedback vertex set number but not for bounded undirected treewidth or bounded undirected feedback vertex set number. To obtain hardness on the latter classes, one would need a nonstandard OMv variant that permits the matrix to be encoded in labels, weights, a fixed low-width grammar, or in the update sequence rather than in arbitrary adjacency. Such a variant would no longer be the usual graph-topological OMv reduction and may conflict with known low-width dynamic-programming algorithms.",
    "delta_genome": "Separate the parameter regimes. First, explicitly state that the Henzinger et al. OMv lower bound already holds for dynamic reachability on DAGs, equivalently directed-FVS number 0. Second, show that the same reduction cannot yield bounded-treewidth or bounded-undirected-FVS hardness because an arbitrary n x n Boolean matrix induces graphs of treewidth \\Omega(n) in the worst case. Third, formulate any possible strengthening only under a stronger 'low-width encoded OMv' hypothesis, where each online matrix is represented by a bounded-treewidth circuit/decomposition but still conjectured to require n^{3-o(1)} total time.",
    "claim_genome": "Under the standard OMv conjecture, the best-supported strengthened lower bound is: fully or partially dynamic directed s-t reachability admits no algorithm with update time u(n) and query time q(n) satisfying n^2 u(n)+n q(n)=O(n^{3-\\varepsilon}) even when all update graphs are DAGs, hence directed feedback vertex set number 0. However, the same OMv framework does not substantiate analogous n^{1-o(1)} update/query lower bounds for graphs of constant underlying treewidth or constant undirected feedback vertex set number; such a claim would require a strictly stronger OMv-like conjecture for low-width encodings and must overcome known dynamic-programming summaries over bounded-width decompositions."
  }
}
```

topic: [v23/math/theoretical_cs] Develop approximation algorithms for submodular maximization under matroid intersection constraints in the streaming model. Analyze whether adaptivity can be re
full_prompt: Develop approximation algorithms for submodular maximization under matroid intersection constraints in the streaming model. Analyze whether adaptivity can be reduced without worsening the competitive ratio beyond current lower bounds.
completion:
Rationale: First locate the closest known results: streaming submodular maximization under multiple matroid-type constraints, and parallel/adaptive lower bounds for submodular maximization.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"streaming monotone submodular maximization matroid intersection p matroid constraints adaptivity lower bounds"}
```  
[result] Simulated: Search returns clusters around (i) threshold/greedy streaming for monotone submodular maximization under cardinality and matroid constraints; (ii) streaming algorithms for \(p\)-matchoids/\(p\)-systems with approximation degrading as \(\Theta(p)\); (iii) adaptivity lower bounds for submodular maximization showing that constant-factor approximation generally requires \(\Omega(\log n/\log\log n)\) adaptive value-query rounds in parallel-query models unless the algorithm uses exponentially many queries; and (iv) matroid-intersection offline algorithms based on exchange graphs/continuous greedy, not directly streaming.

Rationale: The provided candidate is matroid-related; read it to determine whether its space-efficient matroid machinery transfers to submodular streaming or is orthogonal.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W4366087049"}
```  
[result] Simulated: The paper “Scalable and space-efficient Robust Matroid Center algorithms” studies robust clustering/center objectives with matroid constraints. It uses matroid feasibility and coresets/space reduction, but the objective is metric center-style rather than submodular value-oracle maximization. It does not provide a streaming approximation for monotone submodular maximization under matroid intersection, but its space-efficient treatment of matroid independence oracles suggests maintaining compact candidate sets may be viable.

Rationale: Formulate an algorithmic direction by combining thresholded streaming, matroid-intersection exchange feasibility, and batched/low-adaptivity marginal estimation, then compare against known adaptivity lower-bound barriers.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"matroid intersection streaming exchange graph submodular threshold greedy low adaptivity"}
```  
[result] Simulated: Relevant techniques include: maintaining a feasible set under the intersection of \(p\) matroids using local exchange; using geometrically spaced marginal thresholds to avoid knowing \(\mathrm{OPT}\); retaining short exchange certificates/candidate buffers; and reducing adaptivity by evaluating marginal gains for batches against a frozen solution. Known barriers suggest that reducing adaptive rounds below logarithmic or polylogarithmic levels while preserving a constant approximation is unlikely in the general value-oracle model.

propose:
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Design a one-pass threshold-exchange streaming algorithm for monotone submodular maximization over \\(\\mathcal I=\\bigcap_{j=1}^p\\mathcal I_j\\), where each \\(\\mathcal I_j\\) is a matroid accessed by an independence oracle. Maintain \\(O(\\varepsilon^{-1}\\log M)\\) candidate solutions indexed by geometric thresholds \\(\\tau\\), where \\(M\\) brackets \\(\\mathrm{OPT}\\). On arrival of element \\(e\\), compute its marginal \\(\\Delta(e\\mid S_\\tau)\\). If \\(S_\\tau+e\\in\\mathcal I\\) and \\(\\Delta(e\\mid S_\\tau)\\ge\\tau\\), insert it. If infeasible, compute a bounded exchange set \\(X\\subseteq S_\\tau\\) certified by the matroid-intersection exchange graph and insert \\(e\\) only when \\(\\Delta(e\\mid S_\\tau)-\\sum_{x\\in X}\\widehat\\Delta(x)\\ge\\tau\\), deleting \\(X\\). Use lazy marginal upper bounds \\(\\widehat\\Delta(x)\\) to charge discarded value to accepted value. A batched variant freezes \\(S_\\tau\\) for blocks of size \\(B\\), evaluates all marginals in the block nonadaptively, and then applies a deterministic exchange-resolution rule.","niche_genome":"Streaming value-oracle algorithms for monotone submodular maximization under matroid-intersection constraints, especially when \\(p\\) is small or when each arriving element participates in a bounded number of exchange conflicts. The target model is insertion-only, one pass, polynomial independence-oracle calls, and semi-streaming space \\(\\widetilde O(rp/\\varepsilon)\\), where \\(r\\) is the common rank scale.","observation_genome":"For \\(p\\)-matroid intersections, a pure accept-if-feasible threshold algorithm loses value because high-marginal elements arriving late may be blocked by earlier lower-marginal elements. Exchange-based streaming can recover part of this loss: each removed element can be charged to an inserted element, and each inserted element is charged at most \\(p\\) times through the matroid circuits. This suggests an approximation of the form \\((p+1+O(\\varepsilon))^{-1}\\) in favorable exchange regimes, or more conservatively \\(\\Theta(1/p)\\), matching the dependence typical for streaming algorithms over \\(p\\)-systems and \\(p\\)-matchoids. The threshold grid removes the need for knowing \\(\\mathrm{OPT}\\).","limitation_genome":"The proposed algorithm is unlikely to beat known constant-factor lower-bound phenomena for general submodular value-oracle maximization with substantially sublogarithmic adaptivity. In particular, batching marginals against frozen solutions introduces stale marginal estimates; for arbitrary submodular functions, adversarial curvature can make aggressive batching lose a constant factor. Thus one should not expect \\(O(1)\\)-adaptive or fully nonadaptive streaming updates to preserve the best \\(\\Theta(1/p)\\) ratio without additional assumptions such as bounded curvature, random-order streams, or special matroid structure.","delta_genome":"The main technical delta is to replace standard streaming greedy for a single matroid/cardinality constraint by a matroid-intersection exchange filter with amortized submodular charging, while separately parameterizing adaptivity via block size. The analysis would prove a tradeoff: as the number of adaptive marginal-update phases decreases from \\(O(n)\\) to \\(O(\\log n/\\varepsilon)\\) or block-adaptive \\(O(n/B)\\), the approximation degrades by only an additive \\(O(\\varepsilon)\\) term, provided stale marginal errors are controlled by refreshing thresholds geometrically.","claim_genome":"A plausible theorem is: for fixed \\(p\\), there is a one-pass streaming algorithm using \\(\\widetilde O(rp/\\varepsilon)\\) space and polynomial oracle calls that returns \\(S\\in\\bigcap_{j=1}^p\\mathcal I_j\\) with \\(f(S)\\ge (c_p-O(\\varepsilon))\\mathrm{OPT}\\), where \\(c_p=\\Theta(1/p)\\), via thresholded exchange. Moreover, adaptivity can be reduced to polylogarithmic marginal-evaluation rounds by batching and lazy thresholds without asymptotically worsening \\(c_p\\); however, reducing adaptivity below the known \\(\\Omega(\\log n/\\log\\log n)\\)-type value-oracle barriers while keeping a constant approximation would require assumptions beyond general monotone submodularity and arbitrary matroid intersections."}}

topic: [v23/math/theoretical_cs] Analyze the communication complexity of distributed graph coloring in the CONGEST model under adversarial edge insertions and deletions. Establish lower bounds
full_prompt: Analyze the communication complexity of distributed graph coloring in the CONGEST model under adversarial edge insertions and deletions. Establish lower bounds that separate dynamic distributed algorithms from static ones.
completion:
rationale: I will ground the lower-bound target in known CONGEST communication-complexity techniques: reductions from two-party set disjointness/equality via sparse graph gadgets, then adapt them to a dynamic update sequence where the *static* graph after the update is easy to color but deciding/repairing the maintained coloring encodes the hard communication instance.
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"CONGEST lower bounds graph coloring distributed communication complexity dynamic graph algorithms edge insertions deletions"}
```

[result] simulated:
- Found relevant themes: CONGEST lower bounds via two-party communication complexity; distributed graph coloring lower bounds; dynamic distributed algorithms under topology changes.
- Key reusable technique: partition the graph into Alice/Bob sides with an \(O(\log n)\)-edge cut, so every CONGEST round transmits only \(O(\log^2 n)\) bits across the cut.
- Candidate reductions: set disjointness / equality encoded in choices of paths, matchings, or parity constraints.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"conceptual_survey_congest_lower_bounds_dynamic_coloring"}
```

[result] simulated:
- Static CONGEST coloring lower bounds usually prove that computing a legal coloring from scratch requires some number of rounds/messages on carefully constructed graphs.
- Dynamic distributed lower bounds can be stronger when the maintained state before an update hides global information needed after a single adversarial insertion/deletion.
- For coloring, hard instances can be built from parity constraints: maintaining a 2-coloring of a dynamic bipartite graph, or maintaining a \((\Delta+1)\)-coloring with small recourse, can force the algorithm to learn information distributed far away.

propose:
```json
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "gene_genome": {
    "mechanism_genome": "Use a two-party communication reduction embedded into a dynamic CONGEST graph. Partition the network into Alice and Bob subgraphs connected by a sparse cut of size O(1) or O(log n). Alice's input x and Bob's input y determine local edge gadgets on their respective sides. The graph is initially properly colored and locally consistent. An adversarial update sequence consisting of one or O(polylog n) edge insertions/deletions creates a coloring-repair instance whose feasibility or required recoloring pattern reveals whether x and y intersect. Since each CONGEST round communicates only O(log n) bits per cut edge, any dynamic algorithm that stabilizes in T rounds yields a two-party protocol of cost O(T log^2 n), implying T = Omega(CC(DISJ)/log^2 n) = Omega(k/log^2 n) for k-bit set disjointness.",
    "niche_genome": "Dynamic distributed graph coloring in the CONGEST model under fully adversarial edge insertions and deletions, especially algorithms that maintain a legal c-coloring after each update with bounded stabilization time, bounded messages, or bounded recoloring/recourse.",
    "observation_genome": "A single edge insertion can be locally small but globally informative. For example, in a maintained 2-coloring of a dynamic forest or bipartite graph, inserting an edge between two large components requires knowing the parity relation between the endpoints' component color classes. That parity can encode a distributed communication problem. More generally, in a c-coloring gadget, whether a local conflict can be repaired without propagating recolorings across a sparse cut can encode DISJ or EQ.",
    "limitation_genome": "The strongest separation requires specifying the coloring task precisely. For unrestricted (Delta+1)-coloring with arbitrary recoloring and no recourse bound, local randomized algorithms may repair many insertions cheaply. Robust lower bounds should therefore target one of: exact 2-coloring/bipartite coloring, c-coloring for constant c, coloring with bounded recourse, or stabilization after each update against an adaptive adversary. Lower bounds also depend on whether the metric is rounds, total messages, amortized messages, or number of recolored vertices.",
    "delta_genome": "Static coloring from scratch on the final graph may be easy, e.g. the final graph can be a path, forest, or bounded-degree bipartite graph admitting O(log* n) deterministic coloring or polylogarithmic CONGEST coloring. In contrast, the dynamic algorithm starting from a valid maintained state before the adversarial update must distinguish globally different parity/gadget configurations. This yields a dynamic lower bound of Omega(k/log^2 n) rounds for k-bit inputs, producing an asymptotic separation by choosing k = Theta(n). Thus one obtains dynamic stabilization lower bounds near Omega(n/log^2 n) rounds while the corresponding static graph family has much smaller coloring complexity.",
    "claim_genome": "There exists a family of n-node dynamic graphs of constant maximum degree and update sequences of length one or polylog n such that any randomized CONGEST algorithm with constant error that maintains a proper constant-coloring, in particular 2-coloring of bipartite instances or c-coloring of fixed-c gadgets, requires Omega(n/log^2 n) rounds after some adversarial update. The proof is by reduction from two-party set disjointness across a sparse communication cut. This separates dynamic distributed coloring from static coloring on the same final graph family whenever the static CONGEST complexity is o(n/log^2 n), for example O(log* n) or polylogarithmic on paths, forests, or bounded-degree bipartite graphs."
  }
}
```

topic: [v23/math/theoretical_cs] Explore derandomization of randomized incremental algorithms for geometric intersection problems. In particular, seek deterministic near-linear-time algorithms
full_prompt: Explore derandomization of randomized incremental algorithms for geometric intersection problems. In particular, seek deterministic near-linear-time algorithms for low-dimensional arrangements with output-sensitive guarantees.
completion:
rationale: I will first triangulate the relevant derandomization primitives for randomized incremental construction (RIC)—especially Clarkson–Shor sampling, deterministic cuttings, and arrangement construction—then read a small set of canonical papers to extract a concrete mechanism for output-sensitive low-dimensional intersection algorithms.
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"OpenAlex Clarkson Shor random sampling computational geometry deterministic random sampling Chazelle Friedman arrangements output sensitive randomized incremental construction"}
```
[result] simulated:
- Candidate A: Clarkson, K. L.; Shor, P. W. “Applications of random sampling in computational geometry, II.” Discrete & Computational Geometry, 1989/1990. Core source for Clarkson–Shor bounds on arrangements and conflict lists.
- Candidate B: Chazelle, B.; Friedman, J. “A deterministic view of random sampling and its use in geometry.” Combinatorica, 1990. Deterministic replacement for random sampling in geometric range spaces.
- Candidate C: Agarwal, P. K.; Sharir, M. “Arrangements and their applications.” Handbook/survey material on arrangement construction and output-sensitive geometric algorithms.
- Candidate D: Edelsbrunner, H.; Guibas, L. J.; Sharir, M. “The complexity and construction of many faces in arrangements of lines and of segments.” Discrete & Computational Geometry, 1989. Output-sensitive arrangement substructure construction.

rationale: The search suggests the right abstraction is not to derandomize a particular RIC line-by-line, but to derandomize the sampling lemma that makes RIC near-linear: obtain small samples/cuttings with certified conflict-size bounds, then use them to drive a deterministic insertion schedule. I will read the core sampling/cuttings papers and arrangement-construction sources. 
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["openalex:W2144637951","openalex:W2101859057","openalex:W2149417580"]}
```
[result] simulated:
- Clarkson–Shor: For objects with constant description complexity in fixed dimension, random samples yield expected bounds on numbers of low-level cells and on conflict-list sizes. These bounds imply near-linear expected-time RICs for arrangements, lower envelopes, Voronoi diagrams, and related intersection structures.
- Chazelle–Friedman: Random samples in bounded-VC geometric set systems can be replaced by deterministic samples/cuttings using discrepancy/epsilon-net style arguments. The resulting structures preserve the asymptotic sampling guarantees up to logarithmic factors.
- Arrangement-construction literature: For fixed dimension and bounded-degree semi-algebraic objects, arrangement construction is usually output-sensitive in terms of number of realized cells, faces, or intersections, but randomized algorithms hide the key scheduling step in a random permutation.

rationale: The useful gap is therefore: deterministic near-linear algorithms exist for some static cutting primitives, but randomized incremental algorithms often need adaptivity across insertion stages. A proposal should target a deterministic analogue of the random permutation with a verifiable potential function over conflict lists. 
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"deterministic cuttings conflict lists arrangements semi-algebraic objects randomized incremental derandomization output sensitive computational geometry"}
```
[result] simulated:
- Deterministic cuttings and partition trees give near-optimal decompositions for fixed-dimensional hyperplanes, segments, simplices, and many semi-algebraic ranges.
- Existing deterministic methods often add logarithmic overhead and are less modular than RIC.
- Few sources give a general deterministic, output-sensitive replacement for the full adaptive randomized insertion process in low-dimensional arrangements.

propose:
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Derandomize randomized incremental construction by replacing the random permutation with a recursively certified low-conflict insertion schedule. For a fixed-dimensional family F of constant-description-complexity semi-algebraic objects, maintain a vertical/cylindrical decomposition D_i of the current subarrangement and conflict lists C(τ) for cells τ. At phase i, compute deterministically an r-cutting or epsilon-approximation for the range space induced by cells versus remaining objects, then select a batch B_i whose insertion minimizes the Clarkson-Shor potential Φ_i = Σ_{τ∈D_i} binom(|C(τ)|,≤d+1) or a weighted shallow-conflict variant. The selection can be implemented by conditional expectations over a bounded-independence sample space, or by deterministic reweighting using epsilon-approximations. Insert B_i, refine only cells with nonempty conflicts, and rebuild local conflict lists. The target invariant is that total refinement work over all phases is O((n+k) polylog n), where k is the output size of the requested arrangement/intersection structure.","niche_genome":"Low-dimensional geometric intersection problems: arrangements of line segments, algebraic arcs, pseudo-segments, hyperplanes, simplices, balls, or constant-degree semi-algebraic patches in fixed dimension d. The most promising cases are planar segment arrangements, 3D surface arrangements with bounded description complexity, many-faces subarrangements, lower envelopes, and batched intersection reporting where the output parameter k is the number of realized vertices, edges, cells, or reported intersections.","observation_genome":"Randomized incremental algorithms succeed because a random prefix is, with high probability or in expectation, a good geometric sample: it induces few low-level cells and small expected conflict lists. Clarkson-Shor analysis is essentially a statement about low-order moments of conflict ranges. Those moments can often be certified deterministically by cuttings, epsilon-nets, epsilon-approximations, shallow cuttings, or bounded-independence constructions. Thus the randomness needed by RIC is not full independence of a permutation; it is the existence of stagewise samples satisfying explicit conflict-potential inequalities.","limitation_genome":"A fully general derandomization may fail without strong assumptions: fixed dimension, bounded description complexity, availability of deterministic primitive operations, and bounded union/arrangement complexity. Degenerate inputs require symbolic perturbation or simulation of simplicity. Deterministic cuttings may introduce extra logarithmic factors, and maintaining exact conflict lists can dominate the running time unless the decomposition is local and output-sensitive. For high-dimensional or arbitrary semi-algebraic set systems, VC-type guarantees alone may not give near-linear construction time.","delta_genome":"The proposed contribution is a generic deterministic RIC framework whose analysis mirrors Clarkson-Shor but replaces expectation over random permutations by an explicit phase invariant: each deterministic batch must satisfy a low-conflict potential bound relative to the current decomposition. This would unify deterministic cuttings with adaptive incremental construction and give a reusable recipe for deterministic O((n+k) polylog n) algorithms in fixed-dimensional arrangement and intersection problems, rather than isolated deterministic algorithms for individual primitives.","claim_genome":"For fixed dimension d and constant-complexity objects admitting deterministic r-cuttings with linear or near-linear size and computable conflict lists, there should be a deterministic output-sensitive arrangement/intersection algorithm running in O((n+k) log^c n) time and O((n+k) log^c n) space, for a constant c depending only on d and the object family. The proof obligation is to show that the deterministic batch-selection rule enforces the same conflict-list summability as randomized incremental construction: Σ_i Σ_{τ refined at phase i} |C_i(τ)| = O((n+k) log^c n)."}}

topic: [v23/math/theoretical_cs] Investigate whether recent algebraic techniques for matrix multiplication yield improved algorithms for all-pairs shortest paths over small integer weights. Cha
full_prompt: Investigate whether recent algebraic techniques for matrix multiplication yield improved algorithms for all-pairs shortest paths over small integer weights. Characterize barriers implied by min-plus product conjectures.
completion:
Rationale: I will separate (i) “ordinary” algebraic matrix multiplication improvements, which affect APSP only through reductions to ordinary products for bounded weights, from (ii) genuinely min-plus algebra, where APSP is subcubic-equivalent to min-plus product and conjectural barriers apply. I will first locate the relevant APSP/min-plus literature, then cross-check the provided lower-bound candidate for the style of fine-grained barriers.
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"bounded integer weights APSP min-plus product ordinary matrix multiplication Zwick bridging sets Alon Galil Margalit all pairs shortest paths small integer weights"}
```
[result] simulated: Search identifies the classical algorithmic line: Alon–Galil–Margalit on APSP via witnesses and matrix multiplication; Shoshan–Zwick on APSP with integer weights; Zwick’s bridging-sets/rectangular-matrix-multiplication framework; and later refinements using the current ordinary matrix multiplication exponent \(\omega\). Key fact recovered: min-plus products with entries in a small integer range can be reduced to a bounded number of ordinary ring products by encoding weights as exponents, giving running times of the form \(\widetilde O(W n^\omega)\) or related rectangular-MM bounds, depending on the precise weight model.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W4309132721","focus":"fine-grained lower-bound style for graph distance problems and relation to additive-combinatorial/min-plus barriers"}
```
[result] simulated: The paper is not directly about exact APSP over small integer weights; it concerns stronger 3SUM-based lower bounds for approximate distance oracles. It is useful mainly as contextual evidence that graph-distance data structures often inherit fine-grained barriers from algebraic/additive problems. For exact APSP, the more central barrier is the min-plus product/APSP conjectural equivalence rather than 3SUM.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"min-plus product conjecture APSP equivalence truly subcubic bounded weights small integer weights"}
```
[result] simulated: Search recovers the standard fine-grained picture: exact APSP in directed graphs with polynomially bounded integer weights is subcubic-equivalent, up to polylogarithmic/randomized reductions, to min-plus matrix multiplication. The min-plus product conjecture asserts that no \(O(n^{3-\varepsilon})\) algorithm exists for arbitrary \(n\times n\) min-plus product with polynomially bounded integer entries. This conjecture rules out a general truly subcubic exact APSP algorithm for polynomial-weight directed graphs, but it does not preclude faster algorithms under additional structure such as constant or very small edge weights, bounded differences, planarity, sparsity, or approximation.

propose:
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Recent improvements in ordinary algebraic matrix multiplication, i.e. reductions in the exponent \\(\\omega\\), transfer to exact APSP over small integer weights only through reductions of bounded-range min-plus operations to ordinary ring products. For matrices with entries in a range of size \\(W\\), the standard encoding \\(a\\mapsto x^a\\) converts a bounded min-plus product into coefficient/exponent extraction from ordinary products, yielding algorithms of the schematic form \\(\\widetilde O(W n^\\omega)\\), with variants using rectangular matrix multiplication and bridging sets. Thus, if edge weights are constant or sufficiently small, current algebraic matrix multiplication gives genuinely subcubic exact APSP, roughly \\(\\widetilde O(W n^\\omega)\\) or comparable bounds.","niche_genome":"The positive result applies to directed or undirected APSP instances whose integer weights are bounded by a small parameter \\(W\\), especially constant or slowly growing \\(W\\). It is not a generic consequence of faster ordinary matrix multiplication for arbitrary weighted APSP, because shortest-path distances may range over \\(\\Theta(nW)\\) and the relevant closure computation is min-plus rather than ring-linear.","observation_genome":"The algorithmic improvement from recent algebraic techniques is therefore mostly parametric: replacing older values of \\(\\omega\\) by the current best ordinary matrix multiplication exponent marginally improves the exponent in bounded-weight APSP algorithms. However, no known purely algebraic improvement to ordinary matrix multiplication collapses general min-plus product to ordinary multiplication with only polylogarithmic overhead.","limitation_genome":"The min-plus product conjecture, and the fine-grained equivalence between exact APSP and min-plus matrix multiplication for polynomially bounded integer weights, forms the main barrier. If arbitrary min-plus product requires \\(n^{3-o(1)}\\) time, then exact APSP with general polynomial integer weights also cannot admit a truly subcubic algorithm. This barrier does not contradict subcubic algorithms for constant-weight or otherwise structured cases, because those instances avoid the full expressive power of arbitrary min-plus matrices.","delta_genome":"The key distinction is between small edge weights and arbitrary min-plus matrix entries. Bounded edge weights permit algebraic speedups because local weight values are drawn from a small alphabet, while arbitrary min-plus product allows independent polynomial-size entries and encodes the full APSP-hard operation. Hence recent algebraic matrix multiplication advances improve the bounded-weight regime quantitatively but do not change the qualitative conjectural boundary for general exact APSP.","claim_genome":"Recent algebraic matrix multiplication techniques yield improved exact APSP algorithms for sufficiently small integer weights only in the bounded-range/structured regime, with running times parameterized by \\(W\\) and \\(\\omega\\), e.g. \\(\\widetilde O(W n^\\omega)\\)-type bounds. Under the standard min-plus product conjecture, these techniques cannot be expected to produce a truly subcubic exact APSP algorithm for arbitrary polynomially bounded integer weights; any such algorithm would imply a truly subcubic algorithm for general min-plus matrix multiplication and would refute the conjecture."}}

- `oa:W4226300323`: quantum-advantage benchmarking/linear cross-entropy; no direct machinery for Loewner-order certification of sketching algorithms.

The computational phase uses leverage-score or ridge-leverage sampling, CountSketch/SRHT embeddings, or graph-sparsification sampling. The certification phase represents the two PSD inequalities as universally quantified quadratic-form claims over x, namely x^T M_- x \u2265 0 and x^T M_+ x \u2265 0, where M_- = A^T S^T S A - (1-\u03b5)A^T A and M_+ = (1+\u03b5)A^T A - A^T S^T S A. Instead of emitting dense Cholesky factors, the prover emits a structured proof: sampled-row identities, exact rational weights, hash seeds, and a sumcheck/GKR-style certificate reducing quadratic-form verification to near-linear-time evaluations of A, S, and their transposes. For graph Laplacians, the certificate may additionally exploit incidence structure and certify L_H \u2248_\u03b5 L_G via effective-resistance sampling plus Laplacian quadratic-form checks.",

The key connection is that spectral approximation is equivalent to a family of quadratic-form inequalities, and those inequalities can be encoded as algebraic claims over structured matrices generated by the sketch.",

This yields machine-checkable spectral guarantees for least-squares solvers, preconditioners, sparsifiers, and low-rank approximations derived from the sketch."

topic: [v23/math/theoretical_cs] Study the complexity of learning deterministic finite automata with membership and equivalence queries when counterexamples are noisy or adversarially truncated
full_prompt: Study the complexity of learning deterministic finite automata with membership and equivalence queries when counterexamples are noisy or adversarially truncated. Formalize robust convergence guarantees and lower bounds.
completion:
rationale: I will anchor the trajectory in classic exact DFA learning via membership/equivalence queries, then identify which parts survive when equivalence counterexamples are corrupted. The key technical issue is that arbitrary truncation destroys the certificate property “returned word separates target DFA and hypothesis”; exact membership queries let us validate candidates, but cannot force an adversary to reveal a useful distinguishing suffix/prefix.
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"oa:W2995574694","purpose":"Check whether the supplied candidate is relevant background for query-learning complexity or robust DFA identification."}
```
[result] Simulated read: The candidate is a broad thesis/book-like work on computational notions of entropy, including classical/quantum applications. It is not directly about Angluin-style active automata learning, counterexample processing, or DFA query complexity. It may be tangentially useful only if one models noisy counterexamples information-theoretically, but it is not a primary source for this problem.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"Angluin L* deterministic finite automata membership equivalence queries counterexamples noisy adversarial truncation robust lower bounds","domain":"theoretical_cs","limit":5}
```
[result] Simulated search: Core relevant lines are: Angluin’s exact learning model for regular sets from membership and equivalence queries; Rivest–Schapire/Kearns–Vazirani refinements for counterexample processing; active automata learning under imperfect teachers/oracles; statistical/noisy query learning. No standard model appears to give tight guarantees specifically for adversarially truncated equivalence counterexamples, suggesting a formal model and bounds contribution.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"learning DFA malicious equivalence queries noisy counterexamples membership queries lower bound","domain":"theoretical_cs","limit":5}
```
[result] Simulated search: Known exact-query upper bounds rely on every negative equivalence query returning a genuine distinguishing word. With exact membership queries, spurious noisy counterexamples can be filtered by testing whether target(w) differs from hypothesis(w). However, if every genuine counterexample may be replaced by an arbitrary proper prefix, the learner may receive no validated separator, so exact identification is impossible without a quantitative usability assumption.

propose:
```json
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "gene_genome": {
    "mechanism_genome": "Model active DFA learning with exact membership queries MQ_T(w)=T(w) and corrupted equivalence queries CE_T(H). If H is incorrect, the oracle samples or chooses a true separator x with T(x) != H(x), but returns either x or a corrupted word c obtained by noise/truncation. The learner validates every returned c by one membership query and accepts it only if T(c) != H(c). Define a counterexample oracle to be p-usable if, for every incorrect hypothesis H reachable by the learner, each equivalence query returns a validated separator with conditional probability at least p, independently or against an adaptive adversary satisfying a martingale lower bound. Then any standard exact learner such as L* or Kearns-Vazirani can be wrapped by rejection/validation to simulate exact equivalence queries with geometric overhead O(1/p).",
    "niche_genome": "The target niche is robust active learning of regular languages under imperfect teachers: exact MQs but unreliable EQ counterexamples, especially adversarial truncations that may turn a genuine distinguishing word into a non-distinguishing prefix. This sits between classical Angluin exact identification and statistical/noisy query learning, with a focus on query complexity and impossibility thresholds.",
    "observation_genome": "A returned equivalence counterexample is useful only if it remains a separator between the target DFA T and the current hypothesis H. Exact membership queries make separator validation trivial: compute T(c) by MQ and H(c) internally, then discard c if T(c)=H(c). Therefore arbitrary label noise or spurious strings affect complexity only through the probability of obtaining a validated separator. By contrast, adversarial truncation can remove the first distinguishing transition or distinguishing suffix; a prefix of a true separator need not be a separator. Thus truncation is harmless only under an explicit retention/usability condition.",
    "limitation_genome": "Without a lower bound p>0 on the probability of receiving a validated separator, convergence is impossible: an adversary may always return epsilon, or any fixed prefix on which T and H agree, whenever H is wrong. The transcript then becomes indistinguishable from one in which the equivalence oracle gives no negative information beyond repeated rejection. Membership queries alone cannot identify an arbitrary n-state DFA over an infinite word domain with finite worst-case guarantees unless the learner already queries a complete distinguishing set.",
    "delta_genome": "Formalize two robust regimes. In the stochastic regime, if each failed equivalence query yields a true separator with probability at least p, then an exact DFA learner using Q_EQ exact equivalence queries and Q_MQ membership queries converges with probability at least 1-delta using O((Q_EQ/p) log(Q_EQ/delta)) corrupted equivalence calls and Q_MQ+O((Q_EQ/p) log(Q_EQ/delta)) membership calls for validation. For L*, this gives polynomial robust complexity, e.g. O((n/p) poly(|Sigma|,n,m,log(1/delta))) equivalence attempts plus the usual table-closure membership cost, where n is the number of target states and m bounds usable counterexample length. In the adversarial-truncation regime, require an alpha-retention condition: for every returned truncation c of a true separator x, Pr[T(c) != H(c)] >= alpha, or more generally require that a separator prefix be returned with probability at least alpha. Then the same wrapper yields overhead O(1/alpha).",
    "claim_genome": "Robust exact identification of minimal n-state DFAs with exact membership queries and corrupted equivalence counterexamples admits a sharp qualitative threshold: it is polynomially reducible to classical exact learning iff the corrupted equivalence oracle has inverse-polynomial usability probability for validated separators; if usability can be zero under adaptive adversarial truncation, no learner has a finite worst-case convergence guarantee. Quantitatively, validation plus repetition transforms any exact DFA learner into a high-probability robust learner with multiplicative equivalence-query overhead Theta(1/p), and this overhead is information-theoretically optimal because each attempt reveals a usable separator with probability at most p."
  }
}
```

topic: [v23/math/theoretical_cs] Analyze the expressive power of fixed-point logics with counting on classes of sparse random graphs. Determine whether definability thresholds align with algori
full_prompt: Analyze the expressive power of fixed-point logics with counting on classes of sparse random graphs. Determine whether definability thresholds align with algorithmic tractability thresholds for associated decision problems.
completion:
rationale: The supplied candidate is outside finite model theory/random graphs, so I first screen it and then search for the relevant technical anchors: FPC ≈ polynomial-time uniform Weisfeiler–Leman refinements, first/fixed-point zero-one/limit laws for sparse random graphs, and known descriptive-complexity capture results on structurally sparse classes.
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["oa:W7104434574"],"purpose":"screen provided candidate for relevance to fixed-point logics with counting and sparse random graphs"}
```
[result] simulated: `oa:W7104434574`, “Neural architectures for compositional generalisation,” concerns neural network generalization. No substantive connection to finite model theory, fixed-point logic with counting, Weisfeiler–Leman equivalences, sparse Erdős–Rényi graphs, or descriptive complexity. Exclude from technical basis.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"fixed-point logic with counting sparse random graphs zero-one laws Weisfeiler Leman descriptive complexity bounded treewidth nowhere dense random graphs","domain":"theoretical_cs","top_k":5}
```
[result] simulated: Relevant anchors identified:
1. Immerman–Vardi theorem: inflationary fixed-point logic captures PTIME on ordered finite structures.
2. Cai–Fürer–Immerman: limitations of fixed-point logic with counting; connection to bounded-variable counting logic and Weisfeiler–Leman indistinguishability.
3. Finite-model-theoretic zero-one/limit laws for sparse random graphs `G(n,p)` with `p=n^{-α}`, especially dependence on rational/irrational `α`.
4. Grohe-style descriptive complexity results: FPC captures PTIME on several structurally sparse graph classes, e.g. bounded treewidth and related decomposable classes, but not on arbitrary graph classes.
5. Algorithmic sparse-random-graph thresholds: emergence of giant component around `p≈1/n`, `k`-core thresholds, orientability/SAT/CSP thresholds, and tractability changes tied to local tree-likeness, bounded cores, or expansion.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["finite-model-theory:FPC-CFI","random-graphs:sparse-zero-one-laws","descriptive-complexity:FPC-bounded-treewidth"],"purpose":"extract mechanisms governing definability thresholds and compare with algorithmic phase transitions"}
```
[result] simulated:  
- FPC is invariant under counting-bisimulation/Weisfeiler–Leman-style equivalence and is strong on graphs admitting canonical decompositions of bounded width.  
- Sparse `G(n,n^{-α})` has local neighborhoods that are tree-like for many regimes; FO and extensions often exhibit limit-law behavior depending on `α`, but rational critical exponents create definability-sensitive subgraph appearances.  
- Algorithmic tractability thresholds for decision problems on sparse random graphs typically depend on global structure: component sizes, cores, expansion, rank of constraint matrices, or emergence of giant/complex components.  
- Therefore definability thresholds are expected to coincide with algorithmic thresholds only when the algorithmic transition is witnessed by FPC-definable local or canonically decomposable structure. They need not coincide for transitions requiring global symmetry breaking, arbitrary ordering, non-canonical choices, or high-dimensional linear-algebraic invariants not expressible in fixed bounded-variable counting fragments.

{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Model FPC on sparse Erdős–Rényi graphs G(n,p), especially p=n^{-α} and p=c/n, through its equivalence with uniform counting refinements/Weisfeiler–Leman indistinguishability. The operative mechanism is that FPC can define bounded-depth local neighborhoods, count definable equivalence classes, and exploit canonical low-width decompositions, but cannot in general impose arbitrary orders or distinguish Cai–Fürer–Immerman-type parity/symmetry obstructions.","niche_genome":"Sparse random graph regimes where local weak limits are Galton–Watson trees, components are subcritical or unicyclic, or the 2-core/k-core has a sharply changing but statistically regular structure. The most promising classes are subcritical G(n,c/n) with c<1, bounded-treewidth-with-high-probability fragments, and regimes below the emergence of complex cores for CSP-like decision problems.","observation_genome":"Definability thresholds and algorithmic tractability thresholds should not be expected to align universally. They align when the algorithmic threshold is governed by FPC-definable structure, such as component size distribution below criticality, bounded treewidth, local forbidden patterns, or canonically definable cores. They can diverge when tractability depends on global algebraic, parity, expansion, or non-canonical choice phenomena: a problem may remain polynomial-time decidable above a random-graph phase transition while not being uniformly FPC-definable on the induced random class.","limitation_genome":"The claim is asymptotic and distributional rather than worst-case over all sparse graphs. It also depends on the chosen random model and probability scale. For p=n^{-α}, rational α may induce exceptional finite-subgraph threshold behavior, while for p=c/n the critical window around c=1 requires separate treatment. FPC with rank operators or an ambient order would change the boundary substantially.","delta_genome":"Instead of asking whether FPC captures all polynomial-time properties on sparse random graphs, separate three thresholds: (i) local definability thresholds for subgraph/neighborhood properties, (ii) canonical-structure thresholds where FPC can exploit decompositions or cores, and (iii) algorithmic thresholds for the associated decision problems. Compare them via high-probability FPC interpretations of the random graph’s component/core decomposition.","claim_genome":"For sparse random graph classes, FPC-definability thresholds generally refine but do not coincide with algorithmic tractability thresholds. Coincidence occurs in locally tree-like or canonically decomposable regimes where the decision problem reduces, with high probability, to bounded-width counting-definable data. Separation occurs once the relevant polynomial-time algorithm uses global non-canonical choices, linear-algebraic invariants, or expansion/core phenomena not uniformly recoverable in FPC without an order or stronger counting/rank operators."}}
